gaps.title: pi-context CLI surfaces camelCase op-schema keys as flags verbatim — no kebab-case normalization, so conventional --dry-run / --id fail
gaps.description: parseOpArgs (cli.ts:184-233) derives each op's accepted flag names 1:1 from its typebox parameter property keys; a token whose stripped name is absent from the schema throws `unknown flag` (exit 2). Op-schema keys are camelCase, so the conventional kebab-case flag a user types fails: `pi-context update --dry-run` -> `unknown flag: --dry-run` (only `--dryRun` works), although FEAT-006, FGAP-050/051/060, TASK-038/039 and standard CLI convention all write it `--dry-run`. The CLI has no kebab->camel normalization or alias layer. FGAP-032 is the id-flag-specific instance of this same root (--id vs --itemId/--taskId across ops); THIS gap is the general flag-casing/normalization defect, of which the headline update --dry-run failure is the most visible case.

evidence.reference: parseOpArgs derives flags verbatim from camelCase typebox keys; no kebab->camel normalization; an unknown stripped name throws UsageError (exit 2)
the update op declares parameter key dryRun (camelCase) -> CLI flag --dryRun; the conventional --dry-run is rejected
runtime: `pi-context update --dry-run` -> error: unknown flag: --dry-run, exit 2 (only --dryRun is accepted)

gaps.impact: The headline update feature's safety-preview flag fails when typed in the conventional kebab form documented across the substrate; every camelCase op parameter inherits the same footgun. A first-run and dogfooding UX defect on the highest-visibility surface.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Normalize flag matching in parseOpArgs so a kebab-case flag maps to its camelCase op-schema key (accept --dry-run for dryRun, etc.), accepting both forms. Resolve as one flag-normalization layer together with FGAP-032 (the id-flag-specific instance). No op-schema change.
gaps.closed_by: VER-029 / TASK-015 — a kebab->camel alias layer in parseOpArgs resolves conventional kebab-case flags (e.g. update --dry-run) to the camelCase op-schema key; the camelCase form still works.

gaps.title: No op materializes a newly-declared block's data file into an already-installed substrate — neither update nor the install-ceremony write that declared it (a dangling block_kind declaration)
gaps.description: A block_kind added to the catalog after a substrate was installed leaves that substrate with a dangling declaration: config declares the kind and its schema may be present, but its data file is never created, and no op materializes it. updateContext propagates schemas + config-registries only and contains zero block-data-file operations (index.ts:1818-2151; mergeCatalogRegistries scope is the four keyed-array registries relation_types/invariants/block_kinds/lenses, while installed_schemas/installed_blocks are out of scope). installContext materializes block data files only for the install-time installed_blocks set (index.ts:1299-1357). No materialize/ensure/repair-blocks op exists. context-bootstrap-state defines installed as every installed_blocks entry having its data file on disk (context-sdk.ts:397-413; findUnmaterializedAssets context.ts:612-620), so a declared-but-unmaterialized block forces state not-installed. Live triggering instance: the milestone block_kind is in config.block_kinds + installed_blocks and .context/schemas/milestone.schema.json is present, but .context/milestone.json was never created (the install-ceremony closure commit 038c2dc wrote config + schema + object-store only), so context-bootstrap-state reports not-installed with missing.blocks:[milestone] and the milestone kind is unusable (append-block-item --autoId throws on the absent data file). General class: catalog evolution (a new block_kind / schema / vocabulary) has no propagation path to bring an already-installed substrate current — sibling of FGAP-094 (relation_type drift) and FGAP-095/096/097/099 (config/block schema-evolution: load-time migration, repair path, expand-contract discipline, schema_version envelope).

evidence.reference: deriveBootstrapState: state not-installed iff missing.blocks non-empty
findUnmaterializedAssets: a block is missing iff <root>/<name>.json absent (installedBlockDestPath 599-602)
installContext materializes block data files only over the install-time installed_blocks set
updateContext: schemas + config-registries only (mergeCatalogRegistries 2118-2148); zero block-data-file operations
the install-ceremony closure wrote .context/config.json + schemas/milestone.schema.json + object store; NOT .context/milestone.json — the live not-installed origin
full investigation: detector trace, install/update source, live repro, prior-art + class

gaps.impact: Any catalog-added block kind silently bricks the bootstrap signal of every pre-existing substrate: context-bootstrap-state reports not-installed though the substrate is fully usable for its existing blocks, and the new block kind cannot be filed to (its data file does not exist). The canonical 'is this substrate installed' signal lies to every consumer — operator, CI gate, fresh-checkout bootstrap check, readiness-verifying agent. Live on .context since 2026-06-16 (the milestone declaration).
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: block_kind data-file materialization into an already-installed substrate (declaration + schema + starter, on any post-install currency op, not only update)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Provide a sanctioned op that materializes any declared-but-absent block data file into an existing substrate (the missing.blocks[] set from findUnmaterializedAssets), copying the catalog starter via the install path — invoked by update for catalog-added block kinds AND available standalone as a repair/materialize op; surfaced + previewed by --dryRun. Scope is broader than update: the milestone live state was produced by the install-ceremony declaration write, not by update, so the fix is to materialize missing block files on any post-install currency op, not only on update. Verification: a substrate whose config declares a block kind with no data file reports not-installed, the op materializes <kind>.json, and context-bootstrap-state then reports ready.

gaps.title: pi-context update --dryRun reports a version-bump schema as the would-act `resynced` set, not its precise migrate/block outcome — the preview can over-report
gaps.description: Under --dryRun, updateContext's catalog-ahead arm short-circuits BEFORE resyncSchema (no write) and reports the schema under `resynced` as the would-act set, because the concrete resynced/migrated/blocked outcome is unknowable without running the forward migration. So a version-bump schema that a live run would `migrate` (items forward-migrated) — or `block` (no safe chain, or migrated items fail revalidation) — is previewed as `resynced`. The would-register migration declarations (migrationsRegistered) are computed accurately read-only, but a live blocked run rolls those declarations back, so --dryRun can also list migrationsRegistered a live run would not keep. This undercuts FEAT-006's stated guarantee that --dry-run reports the full set of changes update would make: the per-schema OUTCOME under dryRun is optimistic, not exact, precisely when a schema is at risk of blocking.

evidence.reference: updateContext catalog-ahead dryRun branch pushes name onto result.resynced (the would-act set) and computes migrationsRegistered read-only; the live resynced/migrated/blocked classification and the block-path rollback of migrations.json are not modelled under dryRun

gaps.impact: A user previewing an update sees `resynced` for a schema a live run would actually migrate or refuse (block), and migrationsRegistered may list decls a blocked live run would roll back — the dry-run preview can over-state what an update would do, exactly for the schemas most at risk.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Under --dryRun compute the precise per-schema version-bump outcome READ-ONLY (find the catalog migration chain, forward-migrate the populated block in memory, AJV-validate against the new schema) and classify resynced vs migrated vs blocked — reporting migrationsRegistered only for non-blocked outcomes — without writing, so the preview matches a live run.
gaps.closed_by: VER-038 / TASK-046 — update --dryRun predicts the precise catalog-ahead outcome (resynced/migrated/blocked) via simulateResyncOutcome: an in-memory forward-migration + AJV re-validation against the catalog schema, classifying by the live resyncSchema logic, writing nothing; a dry-blocked schema reports no would-register migration declarations. resyncSchema and the live arm byte-unchanged.

gaps.title: pi-context update does not reconcile a catalog registry entry whose body changed against a user-modified entry of the same id (additive-only) — the config-registry analog of the schema 3-way merge
gaps.description: TASK-038/FGAP-060's config-registry propagation is additive-only: an ABSENT catalog entry is appended, but an entry whose identity (canonical_id for relation_types/block_kinds, id for invariants/lenses) is already PRESENT is left exactly as the substrate has it — never reconciled — even when the catalog's body for that same id has changed. So a user who customizes a catalog registry entry's body never receives a later catalog change to that same entry (the user's version is kept entirely; the catalog's body change is silently dropped). This is the config-registry analog of FGAP-046 (the schema clobber/merge gap): there, schema BODIES get a 3-way merge preserving non-conflicting edits; here, config-registry entry bodies have NO merge — additive-only by design (mergeCatalogRegistries, context.ts).

evidence.reference: mergeCatalogRegistries appends only absent entries (by REGISTRY_DESCRIPTORS idField); a present id is never replaced or body-merged, even when the catalog body for that id diverges (additive-only)

gaps.impact: A user who tailors a catalog registry entry (e.g. a relation_type display_name/category, an invariant severity, a lens body) is frozen against future catalog changes to that entry — the value of a catalog registry update is capped for any customized entry, mirroring FGAP-046 for schemas but for config registries.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend update to reconcile a divergent catalog body against a user-modified registry entry of the same id via a 3-way merge (base = the as-installed catalog entry, ours = the substrate entry, theirs = the catalog entry), preserving non-conflicting customizations and surfacing conflicts — the registry analog of the FGAP-046 schema merge. Requires recording a per-registry-entry base (the registry equivalent of base-stamping).

gaps.title: the update conflict resolver spawns a subordinate `pi -p` mergetool instead of returning the conflict to the calling agent — the spawned agent's write-schema is auth-gate-refused (hasUI=false)
gaps.description: On a both-diverged schema CONFLICT, the CLI resolver (resolve-conflicts.ts) dispatches a subordinate pi-bound mergetool via runPiBound(['-p', prompt], …). `pi -p` runs pi NON-interactively (hasUI=false), and write-schema is auth-gated requiring hasUI=true, so the spawned agent's write is REFUSED — it can only print the reconciled schema and tell the operator to run write-schema manually; the reconciliation is never auto-applied. Deeper mis-design: the pi-context CLI is an agent-invoked surface (run by Claude Code or another shell-LLM, rarely a bare-shell human), so the CALLING agent already holds the conflict (the CLI's JSON `conflicts`) + the operator's authority + is interactive — spawning a SUBORDINATE agent to do the reconciliation is the wrong abstraction. DEC-0017's resolver route (a pi-bound agent mergetool that writes via write-schema under the auth-gate) was built for the rare human-at-bare-shell model and is internally inconsistent: the `-p` dispatch makes the session non-interactive, which DEC-0017's own R2 rejection already noted the auth-gate refuses. Surfaced by the live credentialed smoke; TASK-037's structural fake-spawn verification missed it (the fake spawn never ran real `pi -p` against the real auth-gate).

evidence.reference: runPiBound(['-p', prompt], …) dispatches the subordinate pi-bound mergetool; -p is pi's non-interactive print mode → the spawned session is hasUI=false
write-schema is authGated; the auth-gate refuses when hasUI===false, so the spawned agent's reconciled write is refused

live smoke repro: pi-context update --cwd <both-diverged-conflict substrate> → the real agent reconciles but 'write-schema requires interactive user confirmation … ctx.hasUI=false'; rebuild the substrate via /tmp/resolver-smoke-setup.mjs (installContext + a same-node tasks conflict)

gaps.impact: The agent-mergetool resolver never auto-applies a reconciliation. It degrades to: the agent prints the reconciled schema + the operator manually runs write-schema in an interactive pi. The conflict is surfaced and resolvable (never-clobber holds), but the one-command resolver UX is broken for its target case; and for the dominant agent-invoked usage the subordinate spawn is redundant — the calling agent should reconcile + apply.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: caller-as-reconciler (no subordinate-agent spawn for an agent-invoked CLI)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Drop the pi-bound subordinate spawn from the resolver. update returns the typed conflict set (the rendered report + the structured `conflicts`); the calling agent (Claude Code / a shell-LLM) reconciles and applies the result via write-schema under the operator's confirm. Net-simpler code. Amends DEC-0017's resolver route. Re-verify with the live smoke (the calling agent applies the reconciliation end-to-end).
gaps.closed_by: VER-028 / TASK-037 — the update conflict resolver no longer spawns a subordinate pi-bound mergetool; pi-context update surfaces the conflict set (structured under --json + a renderConflicts report) to the calling agent, which reconciles via read-schema/write-schema/resolve-conflict.

gaps.title: a calling agent cannot resolve an update conflict — write-schema does not advance the merge base, so update re-flags a reconciled schema forever
gaps.description: After the FGAP-068 rework, `pi-context update` surfaces a both-diverged schema CONFLICT and the report guides the calling agent to reconcile via write-schema. But following that guidance does NOT resolve the conflict: write-schema writes the resolved body (R) but never advances the merge BASE (config.installed_from.assets[name]), so the next update re-derives the identical 3-way base/ours/theirs disagreement — confirmed empirically (write-schema --yes of the resolved schema, then update --dryRun → the same conflict). The only value that converges is reconciling to the catalog's exact value (ours==theirs). The old (now-deleted) resolver called refreshBaselineForSchema after the agent's write; the rework dropped that with no replacement, leaving the calling agent with no working path to resolve a conflict. Correct semantics (Explore-verified against mergeSchema/checkStatus): a reconciliation means the agent has seen the catalog value (theirs) and chosen R; the new merge base must become the CATALOG body, so checkStatus reads locally-modified (catalogDrift false) and mergeSchema's base==theirs->ours rule (schema-merge.ts:168) takes R on the next update with zero conflicts, R preserved, re-conflicting only when the catalog later changes that path.

evidence.reference: updateContext conflict arm pushes to result.conflicts with NO baseline advance; the brought_current loop (refreshBaselineForSchema) runs only for resynced/migrated/merged, never conflicts — so the base stays stale after the agent's write-schema
mergeSchema atomic rule: base==theirs -> ours; advancing base to the catalog body makes a reconciled schema auto-merge to R with zero conflicts on the next update

empirical repro: pi-context update --cwd <both-diverged substrate> reports the conflict; write-schema --yes the resolved schema; update --dryRun re-reports the identical conflict (base/ours/theirs unchanged)

gaps.impact: The caller-as-reconciler model surfaces conflicts but provides no way to resolve them — a reconciled schema is re-flagged on every update unless the agent happens to pick the catalog's exact value. The conflict-handling guarantee of FEAT-006/update is not met end-to-end without a reconciliation-commit.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: reconciliation-commit (advance merge base to catalog on conflict resolution)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a reflected, auth-gated commit op `resolve-conflict --schemaName <name> [--schema <R>]` that (i) writes R via writeSchemaCheckedForDir if provided, then (ii) advances installed_from.assets[name] (+ object-store stamp) to the CATALOG body (resolveCatalog -> samples/<schema_path>) via a stampBaselineFromBody helper factored from refreshBaselineForSchema. The conflict report's guidance names it. Then update auto-merges the reconciled schema (base==theirs->ours), zero conflicts.
gaps.closed_by: VER-028 / TASK-037 — the reflected auth-gated resolve-conflict op writes the reconciled schema AND advances the merge base to the catalog (stampBaselineFromBody), so a subsequent update converges instead of re-deriving the conflict; verified live end-to-end.

gaps.title: update re-baselines an auto-merged schema to the merged body, not the catalog — so a kept-local divergence is resynced to catalog (clobbered) on the NEXT update
gaps.description: updateContext's post-merge baseline refresh (the brought_current set = resynced ∪ migrated ∪ merged, looped through refreshBaselineForSchema) stamps the baseline from the schema's ON-DISK body. For a 3-way merge that KEPT a local divergence (installed=merged=R, R≠catalog), this stamps base:=R. checkStatus then reads catalog-ahead (catalogDrift = catalog≠base true; installedDrift = installed=R≠base=R false), and the next update's catalog-ahead arm RESYNCS — copyFileSync of the catalog over the installed file — clobbering R. So a kept-local value survives exactly ONE update then is overwritten by catalog on the second. Empirically confirmed via live CLI: resolve-conflict (R=boolean, catalog=string) → update#1 merged, notes.type stays boolean → update#2 resynced, notes.type FLIPS to string → update#3 in-sync string. The class is GENERAL — every path landing a local divergence in result.merged is hit, INCLUDING the plain T3 disjoint auto-merge (the 'both the user's and the catalog's changes survive' guarantee is therefore also only-one-update-durable, not durable). Surfaced through the resolve-conflict (FGAP-069) multi-update demo; the defect is in the FEAT-006 3-way merge baseline (TASK-036), not in resolve-conflict (which stamps base:=catalog correctly). The convergence tests + tests (h)/(i) currently ASSERT the buggy post-merge catalog-ahead state, so the regression ships green.

evidence.reference: brought_current includes result.merged; the post-loop refreshBaselineForSchema (~:1371-1372) stamps from the on-disk merged body (~:1478), making a kept-local merge read catalog-ahead next run
checkStatus catalog-ahead derivation (catalogDrift && !installedDrift); the catalog-ahead arm resyncs via resyncSchema copyFileSync (~:529), overwriting the local divergence
the convergence rule base==theirs->ours: stamping base:=catalog instead makes the next update's merge take R (locally-modified), preserving it durably
test (h) asserts post-merge state catalog-ahead + baseline==merged-body hash — encodes the bug; test (i) ~:1046-1050 likewise; both stop after one update so the clobber is never exercised

gaps.impact: update's never-clobber guarantee fails on the SECOND update: both a reconciled conflict value and a plain disjoint local edit are silently overwritten by the catalog on the next update. The merge 'both survive' / reconciliation 'preserved' guarantees are only-one-update-durable.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: post-merge baseline := catalog (theirs), not the merged body
gaps.proposed_resolution: In updateContext's merge arm, on the conflict-free branch, stamp base := the CATALOG (theirs) body via stampBaselineFromBody (theirs + sourceFile already in scope), !dryRun-guarded; remove result.merged from the post-loop on-disk refreshBaselineForSchema set (resynced/migrated keep it — their on-disk body IS catalog). Then checkStatus reads locally-modified, the next merge takes base==theirs->ours=R, fixed point installed=R/base=catalog stable across updates. Correct tests (h)/(i)/(d)/(j) to assert locally-modified + catalog baseline + multi-update durability.
gaps.closed_by: VER-028 / TASK-037 — updateContext stamps the merge base := the catalog (theirs) body and excludes merged from the on-disk baseline refresh, so a kept-local divergence (reconciled conflict OR disjoint auto-merge) stays locally-modified and survives subsequent updates; live 3x-update + adversarial 4+-update harness confirm a stable fixed point (no resync clobber).

gaps.title: gap-arc-coherence is a review convention but unenforced — no invariant catches an unbound gap, so flat-list drift recurs silently
gaps.description: The gap-arc-coherence convention (conventions.rules) mandates that every framework-gap is bound to a feature-arc or explicitly standalone, but it is enforcement:review only — nothing in context-validate catches an identified gap that shares a surface with sibling gaps yet carries no feature/task binding. The flat-list drift the convention exists to prevent (a cluster of same-surface gaps accreting unbound, half lacking priority, the arc tracked only in prose) therefore recurs silently until a human audit notices. The framework already has the enforcement machinery: FEAT-007's convention-articulation invariants are config-only requires-edge checks (item_governed_by_convention / item_acknowledges_missing_convention on decisions/features/tasks, three requires-edge invariants, no validator code change). The same pattern can enforce gap-arc-coherence's rule 1 for framework-gaps. This is the durability mechanism FEAT-007's governance-enforcement arc is missing for gaps specifically — adjacent to FEAT-007, not covered by it (FEAT-007 enforces item->convention citation; this enforces gap->feature binding).

evidence.reference: gap-arc-coherence rule 1 ('a gap is bound to an arc or explicitly standalone — never an unbound member of a cluster') is enforcement:review, severity:error — a review aspiration, not a checked precondition

FEAT-007's working pattern: relation_types item_governed_by_convention + item_acknowledges_missing_convention (source decisions/features/tasks) + three requires-edge invariants enforce convention articulation config-only; the same shape can require a framework-gap to carry gap_addressed_by_feature / task_addresses_gap OR an explicit standalone-acknowledgment edge

the FEAT-008 reconciliation episode is the worked example: ten same-surface gaps accreted unbound across two vintages, half lacking priority, the arc tracked only in an audit MD + a CLAUDE.md note — exactly what an enforced rule 1 would have caught at filing

gaps.impact: Governance of gap organization depends on a human noticing, not on the validator. Same-surface gaps can accrete unbound indefinitely (item counts only grow), so the substrate drifts back to a flat, mixed-vintage list between audits — the failure gap-arc-coherence was written to end, left unguarded.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: gap-arc-binding invariant (a gap is feature-bound, task-addressed, or explicitly standalone)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add the enforcement as a config-only invariant set, modeled on FEAT-007: (1) a relation_type for an explicit standalone acknowledgment (e.g. gap_acknowledged_standalone, source framework-gaps), so a gap that is intentionally unbound is marked rather than silently orphaned; (2) a requires-edge invariant requiring every NON-closed framework-gap to carry at least one of gap_addressed_by_feature / task_addresses_gap / gap_acknowledged_standalone; (3) severity warning initially (.context has legitimately-unbound gaps today — a backfill pass binds or standalone-marks them before raising to error, mirroring FEAT-007's clean-after-backfill approach). Decide the severity-raise gate + run the backfill as part of landing it. No validator code change — invariants are config-declared. File under the governance arc adjacent to FEAT-007.

gaps.title: pi-context per-op <op> --help lacks the best-of-breed template — no EXAMPLES, SYNOPSIS, RELATED/see-also, or footer; and structured help bypasses --format
gaps.description: deriveHelp(op) (packages/pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts:554) renders a per-op help page consisting only of `${op.name} — ${op.description}`, a Flags: list (each `--field <type> (required|optional) — desc`), and one global-flags line (grep SYNOPSIS|examples|related in cli.ts returns zero matches). Against the R-0011 best-of-breed bar (analysis/2026-06-09-cli-help-ux-research.md §6) for an agent-invoked CLI whose --help is the agent's PRIMARY documentation surface (§5: agents read --help before docs, and learn patterns from examples faster than from flag descriptions), the per-op page is missing the highest-leverage elements: (1) a copy-pasteable EXAMPLES block (the single highest-value agent-help element) — requires a per-op examples[] help-metadata source added to OpDefinition in ops-registry.ts, authored for all ~48 use-ops (seedable from the CLAUDE.md canonical filing patterns); (2) a SYNOPSIS/usage line in docopt notation, derivable from the op's typed params (required -> `--flag <type>`, optional -> `[--flag <type>]`, @file alternation); (3) a RELATED/see-also hint surfacing sibling ops within the op's name-prefix group plus the process-mode pi-bound (undiscoverable from a per-op page today); (4) a learn-more footer. Additionally, help is emitted as raw process.stdout.write in main() (~:644 top-level, ~:674 per-op) and does NOT participate in the --format text|json|table layer (FGAP-021, shipped) — so there is no machine-readable structured-help rendering ({synopsis, summary, flags[], examples[], related[]}) off the registry, which R-0011 §7.3 derived as the target (machine help through the existing --format). This is the PER-OP help-template class, a DISTINCT code location from FGAP-062 (the top-level grouped listing in deriveTopHelp) and FGAP-063 (--version in main): it edits deriveHelp + the OpDefinition help-metadata source + the help-render-through-format path. Already-covered elements NOT in this gap: top-level grouping/promptSnippet/pi-bound-in-listing (FGAP-062), required-vs-optional typed flags (present in deriveHelp), --version (FGAP-063), exit codes (FGAP-026), --format result-render (FGAP-021), field-named AJV errors (FGAP-023), flag-name normalization (FGAP-064/032). Plain-by-default color (R-0011 §7.2) is already satisfied (help is plain text) and is not part of this gap.

evidence.reference: deriveHelp emits `${op.name} — ${op.description}`, a Flags: list, and the global-flags line only — no synopsis line, no examples block, no related/see-also, no footer
main() writes deriveTopHelp()/deriveHelp(op) via process.stdout.write; help output does not route through the --format text|json|table layer (which shapes op RESULTS, not help)
OpDefinition carries promptSnippet/label/description but NO examples/related/synopsis help-metadata source for a per-op help template to render

R-0011 §6 reusable-elements checklist (per-op template: synopsis + flags + EXAMPLES + RELATED + footer) and §5 (agents read --help before docs; examples are the highest-value element); §7.3 (machine help through the existing --format)

gaps.impact: The reflecting CLI is agent-invoked and --help is the agent caller's primary documentation surface. The per-op page gives flags but no examples, no call-shape synopsis, and no related ops, so an agent driving an unfamiliar op cannot see a copy-pasteable invocation, the synopsis, or what to call next — forcing trial-and-error and --help round-trips, and forcing prose-scraping where a structured --format json help would be parseable. FEAT-008 (the best-of-breed CLI) is not best-of-breed until the per-op help page carries examples + synopsis + related; per the standing best-of-breed/no-deferral mandate this is required work, not a deferrable separate decision.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: per-op help template (synopsis + examples + related + footer + machine help via --format)
gaps.proposed_resolution: A per-op help-template render layer, ADDITIVE (in-pi op behavior frozen — run/parameters/description unchanged; the FEAT-008 byte-unchanged invariant is about op BEHAVIOR, and the parity gate TASK-019 asserts op<->CLI behavioral parity, which additive help-metadata does not touch): (1) add an examples[] (and optionally related[]/synopsis-override) help-metadata field to OpDefinition in ops-registry.ts, authoring examples[] for all ~48 use-ops from the CLAUDE.md canonical filing patterns; (2) in deriveHelp (cli.ts:554) render a fixed template — SYNOPSIS (derived from typed params, docopt notation) -> summary -> Flags (existing) -> EXAMPLES -> RELATED -> footer; (3) derive RELATED within the op's name-prefix group and surface pi-bound; (4) route a structured-help rendering ({synopsis, summary, flags[], examples[], related[]}) through the existing --format json so --help participates in --format. Fold into FEAT-008 as a SIBLING task to TASK-040 (different code location per the feature-decomposition rule), decompose per feature-decomposition, verify via the canonical pipeline; the parity gate stays green (additive).
gaps.closed_by: VER-035 / TASK-042 — per-op <op> --help now renders the best-of-breed template (SYNOPSIS + Flags + EXAMPLES + RELATED + footer) via a pure buildHelpModel; OpDefinition gained an additive examples? field authored for all ~50 use-ops; --help --format json emits the structured help model; RELATED derives from the single groupForOp source; the auto-supplied set is single-sourced to AUTO_SUPPLIED (Flags annotate (required; auto-derived/auto-injected)) and the TASK-019 parity gate migrated to read its keys. Additive: in-pi ops frozen, parity gate green.

gaps.title: The reflecting pi-context CLI has no op to project a config-declared target-lens as a binned item-view (the lens-view primitive exists but is reachable only inside a roadmap)
gaps.description: The reflecting CLI has no op to project a config-declared lens as a binned item-view. config.lenses[] declares four target-lenses — gaps-by-status (target framework-gaps, derived_from_field status, bins identified/accepted/in-progress/closed/wontfix), gaps-by-priority, tasks-by-status, features-by-status — none reachable as a view; the gap covers kind=composition and hand-curated lenses too, i.e. the whole config.lenses[] registry. The binning primitives exist: loadLensView (lens-view.ts:43-97) resolves a lens, reads its target items, and groups them into the declared bins (kind target + composition); renderLensView (lens-view.ts:105-150) renders bins as markdown. They are reflected only inside a roadmap — context-roadmap-load/render, keyed by roadmapId via serializeRoadmapView — not by a standalone lensId. The only lensId-keyed op, context-edges-for-lens (ops-registry.ts:1795-1810), returns flat Edge[] (bin as parent, item id as child, paginated, no item bodies, no grouping). Broader class: config-declared vocabulary (lenses, status_buckets, invariants, state-derivation) is under-surfaced as queryable ops; the state-derivation member is FEAT-004, adjacent and not covering lens projection.

evidence.reference: loadLensView(cwd, lensId) resolves a config.lenses[] entry, reads its target block's items, and groups them into the declared bins (kind target 76-96, kind composition 61-74); returns {lens, items, edges, grouped, uncategorized, suggestionTemplate}.
renderLensView(view, naming) renders the loaded view as markdown — bins as headings, items as id/status/title lines.
context-edges-for-lens is the only lensId-keyed op; it returns flat Edge[] (bin as parent, item id as child, paginated, no item bodies, no grouping).
context-roadmap-load/render are the only reflected callers of loadLensView/renderLensView, keyed by roadmapId via serializeRoadmapView, not by a standalone config.lenses[] lensId.

read-config --registry lenses lists four target-lenses (gaps-by-status, gaps-by-priority, tasks-by-status, features-by-status); the per-bin workaround filter-block-items --block framework-gaps --field status --op eq --value <bin> returns one bin and one status-value at a time and exceeds the 50KB read cap on framework-gaps.

gaps.impact: The substrate declares a lens vocabulary and ships the binning/render primitives, but the reflecting CLI — the dogfooding and new-user surface — cannot render a block grouped by status/priority for any declared lens. Seeing open gaps (or tasks/features by status) requires hand-running filter-block-items one bin and one status-value at a time, against a framework-gaps block that exceeds the 50KB read cap. Every declared lens inherits the gap.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: config-declared lens projected as a binned item-view via a reflected op
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a reflected op context-lens-view keyed by --lensId that calls loadLensView (lens-view.ts:43) and returns the binned item-view, with renderLensView (lens-view.ts:105) for the text surface. Serve every config.lenses[] kind (target, composition, hand-curated). Modes: a bin summary (counts, within the cap) and, under --bin, that bin's items paged via pageArray (read-element.ts:188). Route through the boundary read-cap. Additive: a new op; all other in-pi ops byte-unchanged. The op<->CLI parity gate and the per-op help template cover it.
gaps.closed_by: VER-037 / TASK-043 — the additive context-lens-view op projects any config-declared lens as a binned item-view (bin->count summary + per-bin paging via pageArray, routed through structureForRead), reusing loadLensView/renderLensView; serves target/composition/hand-curated lenses; slotted in groupForOp; ops frozen, parity green.

gaps.title: Past-filed context atoms violate rhetorical-register at scale — provenance/process/git narration in block bodies that compose verbatim into subagent contexts
gaps.description: A substrate-wide audit of all 17 block kinds found ~197 rhetorical-register violations across ~118 items. Dominant class: rule 5 (provenance / process / git narration), with rule 1 (declarative-not-narration) co-firing. Three sub-classes by volume: closure/outcome fields used as a changelog (closed_by / resolved_by / notes-at-completion — the largest source); origin/discovery narration; assert-then-refute and in-body corrections. Hedging and first-person are near-absent. The register-guarded block (framework-gaps) has the cleanest bodies, with a recency gradient — post-guard items clean, legacy FGAP-001..033 violating; tasks show the same gradient; unguarded blocks show none. session-notes is excluded: narrative is its purpose (rule 6). Two enforcement gaps: the register checkpoint guard covers only framework-gaps, and no pass cleans the already-filed violating bodies.

evidence.reference: Full audit: ~197 violations / ~118 items across 17 block kinds; per-block tallies (items audited, violating, by severity + by rule#), the violations table (block/id/field/rule/severity/offending quote), and the recency gradient.
35/35 verification items: the evidence field carries git-SHA + probe-loop process narration (rule 5).
~17/17 decisions: the context field carries origin narration (rule 5).
11/11 research items: findings_summary carries in-body corrections / assert-then-refute (rule 5).
2/2 issues: body + resolved_by carry discovery + git narration (rule 5).
closed_by on legacy gaps + bodies of FGAP-001..033 violate; post-guard gaps are clean — the gradient showing the checkpoint guard works where applied.

gaps.impact: Block bodies compose verbatim into subagent contexts — the substrate's stated purpose — so provenance/process/git narration is injected as operating context: noise that dilutes the atomic instruction and can mislead a downstream consumer. The violations concentrate in outcome/closure fields, the fields a consumer reads to learn current state.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: rhetorical-register compliance across filed context atoms
gaps.proposed_resolution: Broaden the register checkpoint guard to the write-heavy blocks (tasks, verification, decisions, issues, research, features; exclude session-notes per rule 6), covering append AND closure/update writes, and clean every existing violating body to declarative current-truth with no signal loss. Tracked by the broadening + remediation task.

gaps.title: check-changelog watched-surface derivation over-includes non-shipping files — build-excluded *.test.ts and monitor examples/*.patterns.json learned-pattern stores
gaps.description: scripts/check-changelog.ts derives a package's watched published surface from package.json files[], mapping the shipped dist/ to the whole src/ tree (watchDirsFromFiles, scripts/check-changelog.ts:56-72). This over-includes files that do not ship: (1) *.test.ts — every tsconfig.build.json excludes **/*.test.ts, so tests never compile to dist/; (2) the behavior-monitor learned-pattern stores packages/pi-behavior-monitors/examples/*.patterns.json (hedge, fragility, commit-hygiene, unauthorized-action, work-quality) — a monitor test rewrites them on each run, and examples/ is in that package's files[]. A change confined to either class is flagged 'published-surface change without a [Unreleased] entry,' demanding a changelog line for a change that never reaches dist/. General class: the dist/->src/ whole-tree watch counts build-excluded files and data-dir files that are not shipped API; the two classes above are its instances.

evidence.reference: watchDirsFromFiles maps a files[] dist/ entry to the whole packages/<pkg>/src/ prefix (or *.ts for root-source packages), with no exclusion for *.test.ts.
exclude includes **/*.test.ts — test files never compile to dist/ and never ship.
files[] ships examples/, so check-changelog watches packages/pi-behavior-monitors/examples/ — including the *.patterns.json learned-pattern stores.
rewritten by a behavior-monitor test run (learned-pattern append), producing a tracked-file diff that the gate counts as a published-surface change.

gaps.impact: A change confined to non-shipping test files or to a monitor learned-pattern store cannot be committed without adding a spurious [Unreleased] line for a change that does not ship. The gate's published-surface model is broader than the shipped dist/ artifact.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: published-surface derivation matching the shipped artifact
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a subtractive isExemptSurface(path) predicate in check-changelog.ts exempting /\.test\.[cm]?tsx?$/ and packages/pi-behavior-monitors/examples/ paths ending in .patterns.json; filter changedPaths through it at the top of changedPackages, before classification. Subtractive and orthogonal — watchDirsFromFiles/pathInWatchSet/unreleasedGrew unchanged; a genuine src/*.ts (non-test) and a real examples/*.monitor.json still flag. Extend check-changelog.test.ts with the exempt-passes and the real-surface-still-flags cases.
gaps.closed_by: VER-036 / TASK-045 — check-changelog exempts non-shipping surface via a subtractive isExemptSurface predicate (build-excluded *.test.ts + monitor examples/*.patterns.json) filtered in changedPackages before classification; watch-set derivation and matching unchanged; a genuine src/*.ts and examples/*.monitor.json still flag.

gaps.title: pi-context update result does not make partial application legible — a blocked run can read as a no-op while config.json was mutated
gaps.description: update applies per-component (a blocked schema rolls back only itself; the additive config-registry propagation writes regardless — affirmed by DEC-0018), but the result does not make that partiality legible. A live run that returns a schema under `blocked` still mutated config.json with the catalog-new registry vocabulary (relation_types/block_kinds/invariants/lenses), yet nothing in the result ties the two together: `blocked` and `registryAdditions` are independent channels, so a caller reading `blocked` can reasonably conclude nothing was applied while config.json in fact changed. General class: update's result reporting does not surface its per-component partial application across the independent seams (schema-loop outcomes + registry propagation); the registry-write-while-a-schema-blocked case is the triggering instance.

evidence.reference: post-loop registry propagation computes registryAdditions and calls writeConfig when !dryRun, ungated on schema-loop outcome — runs even when a sibling schema is in result.blocked

the schema-loop result buckets (resynced/migrated/blocked, populated through ~1908-1960) are reported independently of registryAdditions; no field ties a blocked outcome to the config mutation that still occurred
R-0012 — live reproduction: conventions blocked, registryAdditions (incl. item_derived_from_item) written to config.json in the same run

gaps.impact: A user or agent reading a `blocked` update result can assume the substrate was left unchanged and skip reconciling a config.json that did change — silent partial application. The risk is legibility, not data loss (the additive write is safe); it undermines trust in the result as a faithful account of what update did.
gaps.proposed_resolution: update's result surfaces its partial application explicitly: when any schema is `blocked` (or refused/conflicted) AND registry additions or other-schema resyncs were applied, the result reports the partiality in one place — what was applied (registryAdditions, resynced/migrated schemas) alongside what was refused and why (the blocked schema + its AJV/validation error) — so a `blocked` run never reads as a no-op. Covers both the CLI text surface and the structured result object. Premised on DEC-0018 (per-component affirmed); pairs with FGAP-066 (dry/live outcome parity).
gaps.closed_by: TASK-080 / VER-067 — UpdateResult gains the optional partialApplication field, populated exactly when a run both refused something (blocked / refused / conflicts non-empty) and applied something (resynced / migrated / merged / migrationsRegistered / any registryAdditions array non-empty): applied/notApplied mirrors of the result channels plus a one-line summary naming what was applied alongside what was refused and why, computed last in updateContext as a pure derivation of the finished result for live and dryRun runs (the preview declares itself). A blocked run whose registry additions landed no longer reads as a no-op on either surface (the op returns the result object; text mode renders the same object). Merged c81c06b2; three test cells; promoted-CLI runtime demo shows the field end-to-end; live substrate stays a clean no-op.

gaps.title: No per-item schema-validation diagnostic — which item, field, and constraint fail a schema is reported nowhere
gaps.description: No surface reports which item id, field, and constraint cause a block's items to fail a schema. update's resyncSchema discards the AJV error at an unbound catch (index.ts:622), returning a bare {status:blocked}. context-validate runs referential-integrity + invariant checks only, not per-item schema validation. No op validates a block's items against a target schema and returns the failures. The AJV detail is fully present at the throw site (allErrors:true; instancePath, keyword, params, message) and dropped. An operator facing a blocked schema must hand-validate the block against the catalog schema to learn what failed.

evidence.reference: resyncSchema's catch is unbound — the thrown ValidationError carrying errors[] is discarded; returns {status:blocked, registeredMigrations:[]}
validate compiles with allErrors:true and throws ValidationError holding the full per-item AJV errors[] (instancePath/keyword/params/message)
validateContext checks edge referential integrity + config-declared invariants only; it never AJV-validates block items against their schemas

gaps.impact: A blocked schema is opaque: the operator cannot see which item or field failed, cannot distinguish a real invalid item from a migrate/validate fault, and must reverse-engineer the failure by hand-validating the block.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A returns-not-throws per-item validator — block items against a target schema version yielding per-item {item id, field, keyword, params} — surfaced both as a standalone op and within update's blocked output, mapping instancePath /<arrayKey>/N to the item id. Reuses the AJV errors validate/validateBlockWithMigrationForDir already produce; shares the in-memory migrate+validate primitive with FGAP-066/TASK-046. Distinct from FGAP-076 (partial-application legibility).
gaps.closed_by: VER-039 / TASK-048 — update blocked entries carry the per-item validation diagnostic (UpdateResult.blockedDetail: reason no-migration-chain | validation-failed, the version pair, and per failing item the id/field/constraint, dry and live identically); the standalone validate-block-items op validates a named block against the catalog schema version and returns the per-item failure list without writing; renderBlocked surfaces the detail on the CLI text surface. blocked: string[] and the rollback contract unchanged.

gaps.title: check-status reports no version gap — which schemas are behind the catalog, and by how much, is not surfaced
gaps.description: checkStatus computes baseline_version and catalog_version per asset (CheckStatusAsset), but renderCheckStatus prints only the per-state name buckets and drops the version fields, and check-status is reachable only as an in-pi command (no CLI op — FGAP-030). No surface reports which schemas are behind the catalog or the version delta. A behind-by-version readout must also cover content-only drift: catalog-ahead is a content-hash comparison, so a schema can be behind with no version-number change.

evidence.reference: CheckStatusAsset carries baseline_version + catalog_version per asset
renderCheckStatus groups schema names by state and omits the version fields
checkStatus classifies catalog-ahead by content-hash comparison (catalogHash !== baseline), not version arithmetic

gaps.impact: From check-status the operator cannot see which schemas are behind the catalog or by how much — only the raw drift state, and only from inside pi.
gaps.proposed_resolution: check-status reports, per behind schema, the baseline->catalog version delta, and handles content-only drift (behind with no version-number change). Depends on FGAP-030 (expose check-status as a CLI op).
gaps.closed_by: VER-043 / TASK-049 — checkStatus reports the version gap: additive per-asset behind + version_delta fields (basis version-bump or content-only — catalog-ahead is a hash comparison, so behind at equal versions states the content basis), and renderCheckStatus annotates behind names inline with the delta; the classification arm byte-unchanged.

gaps.title: Catalog schema is not locally diffable — no surface yields the schema body, only a projection
gaps.description: No surface yields the catalog schema body for local diff against an installed schema. read-samples-catalog returns a projection (title, item-shape, relations, invariants, lenses), not the raw JSON Schema (no properties/definitions/$id). The catalog lives under the package at samples/schemas/*.schema.json, i.e. node_modules/@davidorex/pi-context/samples/schemas/ when installed as a dependency.

evidence.reference: buildSampleKind re-projects each schema to title + item-shape; the raw schema body (properties/definitions/$id) is not emitted
resolveCatalog resolves samplesRoot one dir up from the compiled module — node_modules/@davidorex/pi-context/samples/ when installed as a dependency
installedSchemaDestPath writes the installed schema at <substrate>/schemas/<name>.schema.json — the local diff target

gaps.impact: To diff an installed schema against the catalog, the operator must open the package's samples/schemas/ inside node_modules; the reflecting CLI exposes no catalog schema body.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A fetch op emitting the catalog body (path.join(samplesRoot, kind.schema_path), or the object-store base body via getObject). No sidecar.
gaps.closed_by: VER-044 / TASK-050 — the read-catalog-schema op emits the verbatim catalog *.schema.json body for a named kind (raw JSON Schema: properties/definitions/$id), byte-exact and locally diffable against the installed <substrate>/schemas/<name>.schema.json without touching node_modules; no sidecar.

gaps.title: blocked is a dead-end — no persisted state and no command to re-validate, advance the base, and write a corrected schema
gaps.description: A blocked outcome leaves the schema, block, and migrations.json byte-unchanged and records nothing actionable: resyncSchema's rollback restores migrations.json and returns {status:blocked, registeredMigrations:[]}, so the target schema version, the migration chain, and the per-item failure set are not persisted. Unlike a conflict — surfaced as {base,ours,theirs} and committed via resolve-conflict, which advances the merge base — blocked offers no resolution affordance. No command re-validates a corrected block against the target schema, advances the merge base, and writes the new schema.

evidence.reference: resyncSchema blocked path rolls everything back and persists no pending state
resolveConflict + stampBaselineFromBody — the conflict resolution template that commits a reconciliation and advances the merge base to the catalog so it converges
updateContext buckets a blocked schema by name only — no surfaced state, no affordance

gaps.impact: A blocked schema cannot be resolved through the tool: after fixing the items or local schema the operator re-runs full update, with no convergence affordance and no record of what to fix.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A persisted pending-blocked state (target schema body/version, migration chain, per-item failure set) plus a resolve-blocked / update --continue commit op that re-validates the corrected block against the target schema, advances the merge base via stampBaselineFromBody, and writes the schema — mirroring resolve-conflict. Reuses the object store, stampBaselineFromBody, and validateBlockWithMigrationForDir. Pairs with the in-file markers gap.
gaps.closed_by: VER-040 / TASK-051 — a blocked update persists a pending-blocked record (pending-blocked.json + the target schema body pinned in the object store: name/reason/version pair/chain/failures) and the authGated resolve-blocked op commits the resolution: re-validates the corrected block against the pinned target, on pass registers the chain, writes the target schema, advances the merge base (base := target), and clears the entry — a re-run update reports in-sync; on fail it reports the remaining per-item failures and writes nothing. The blocked byte-unchanged contract and per-component application unchanged.

gaps.title: update writes no in-block diagnostic — a blocked schema's failures appear only in a CLI report, not in the block file
gaps.description: On a blocked schema update writes nothing into the block: the failure is visible only in a CLI report, and the blocked contract leaves the block byte-unchanged (index.ts:491-508). The operator opens the block and sees no marking of the offending items or fields.

evidence.reference: the byte-unchanged blocked contract — the block is never written on the blocked path
writeBlockForDir is the whole-block writer; identity-stamping recomputes content_hash and pre-write AJV rejects non-schema marker fields, so in-file markers are not free
putObject/getObject — the as-installed block/schema body is recoverable, so 'no damage' can hold without 'never written'

gaps.impact: A blocked schema's failures are not visible at the point of fix — in the block file — only in a separate report the operator must cross-reference by hand.
gaps.proposed_resolution: git-merge-style failure markers written into the block at the offending items/fields, with the pre-marker block recoverable from the object store. This supersedes the byte-unchanged blocked contract with a marker-mode (the operator's git model: markers in-file, fix, resolve via the blocked resolution loop). Pairs with the blocked resolution loop and DEC-0018.
gaps.closed_by: VER-041 / TASK-052 — git-style in-file failure markers are the default behavior of a live validation-failed block: full-line sentinels at the offending items/fields (string-aware placement), the pre-marker block pinned to the object store and restored byte-exact, resolve-blocked strips on a passing resolution and refuses while the named violations persist, item identity preserved across the round-trip; dry and no-chain runs write nothing; schema and migrations.json byte-unchanged on every path.

gaps.title: Invariant engine cannot express exists-a-complete-advancer rules — no aggregate-over-incoming-edges invariant class
gaps.description: InvariantDecl (context.ts:202-214) declares two classes: requires-edge (edge presence, status-blind) and status-consistency (per-edge comparison of the gated source's when_bucket against one target's own status bucket). Neither can express a rule whose predicate aggregates over an item's INCOMING edges with a status condition on the far endpoints — e.g. the user-stories completion rule: a task or feature can't be complete until every story it advances is met, where met(story) = exists an advancer (via task_advances_story / feature_advances_story) whose status is complete, and the completing item counts as its own story's advancer (single-advancer no-deadlock). status-consistency has no existential quantifier, reads only the single target's own status, and has no self-satisfies construct. Triggering instance: the user-stories block completion rule (analysis/2026-06-10-user-stories-block-DRAFT.md). General class: any invariant of the shape 'source in bucket X requires each related target to satisfy an aggregate over the target's own edges' is inexpressible; advancer-completion is its first required instance.

evidence.reference: InvariantDecl: class is requires-edge | status-consistency; predicates are when_bucket / require_target_bucket / forbid_target_bucket — single-target, no quantifier, no self-reference
the engine audit: named analogs (task-completed-gap-closed etc.) are config data over the two classes; the advancer-completion rule is inexpressible; enforcement binds only at context-validate

gaps.impact: The user-stories completion rule — and any future aggregate-over-incoming-edges invariant — cannot be declared; the rule is unenforced until the engine gains the class.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a config-declarable invariant class (advancer-completion: for a source item entering when_bucket, every target via the declared relation_types must have at least one incoming advancer edge whose endpoint is in met_bucket, with self_satisfies counting the completing item). Config data, not a hardcoded check, per the existing direction (the analogs are declarations). Binds at context-validate as severity error.

gaps.title: --block-taking ops ignore the config-declared prefix->block mapping — neither input normalization nor error enrichment
gaps.description: Ops that take a --block name resolve it verbatim; a wrong-but-derivable name (--block task, --block decision) fails with a bare 'Block file not found' although config block_kinds declares both canonical_id and prefix, and expectedBlockForId (context-sdk.ts:1110) already computes TASK-046 -> tasks from it. The mapping is used neither to normalize the input (accept the singular / derive the block from a kind-prefixed id) nor to enrich the error (name the block that would have worked). resolve-item-by-id exists to bridge kind-id -> block, but the name-scoped readers' failure path never reaches for it. Class: every --block-taking op; triggering instance: read-block-item --block task. Full investigation: analysis/2026-06-10-block-name-vs-kind-id-resolution-gap.md.

evidence.reference: readBlockForDir raises the generic 'Block file not found' without consulting the prefix->block mapping
expectedBlockForId already derives the block from an id's kind-prefix via config block_kinds
agent investigation: root cause, verbatim repro, op enumeration, class verdict, prior-art (extends closed FGAP-032/FGAP-019 normalization precedents to the block-name axis)

gaps.impact: A natural-but-wrong block name costs a failed invocation and a help round-trip on every --block-taking op, although the substrate already holds the data to resolve or explain it.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Leverage the existing prefix->block mapping at the --block resolution layer: enrich the not-found error with the derivable block name (and accept an id-derived block where the op also receives an id). CLI/dispatch-layer change; ops' canonical semantics unchanged. Verification per the investigation's CLI-provable conditions.

gaps.title: CSV shorthand is a one-param special case, not a type-driven normalization for string-array params
gaps.description: Array-typed op params are classified json and parsed with strict JSON.parse (cli.ts:126-142, 388-408); the only CSV affordance is a hardcoded post-loop split gated on field === 'value' AND op === 'in' (cli.ts:415-423, the FGAP-025 closure). So resolve-items-by-id --ids TASK-046,DEC-0018 fails (exit 2, 'not valid JSON') although --ids is Type.Array(Type.String()) (ops-registry.ts:1746) and the CSV intent is unambiguous. --ids is currently the sole string-array param on the use-op surface; all other array-shaped params (--edges, --item, --match, ...) are object/record arrays a CSV transform must not touch. Class: CSV-for-string-arrays should be type-driven, covering --ids and every future string-array param; the one-param special case is the instance pattern. Full investigation: analysis/2026-06-10-array-param-csv-shorthand-gap.md.

evidence.reference: array params parsed with strict JSON.parse; CSV re-throws as UsageError
the FGAP-025 CSV split is gated on field === 'value' AND op === 'in' — param-specific, not type-driven
resolve-items-by-id ids is Type.Array(Type.String()) — the sole string-array use-op param
agent investigation: root cause, verbatim repro (hit by two independent agents), full array-param enumeration, class verdict, FGAP-025 scope quoted

gaps.impact: The natural CSV form fails on string-array params with a JSON-parse error; every future string-array param inherits the footgun unless normalization is type-driven.
gaps.proposed_resolution: In parseOpArgs, when a param's schema is an array of strings and the value is not valid JSON, split CSV to a string array — type-driven, covering --ids and future string-array params; object-array params and the JSON form unchanged. Verification per the investigation's CLI-provable conditions.

gaps.title: update-block-item cannot delete a field — a null update sets the key to null instead of removing it
gaps.description: update-block-item --updates '{"field":null}' sets the field to null on the item rather than removing the key; no op surface deletes a field from an item. The observed workaround is upsert-block-item full-shape replacement. Hit removing a stray status field from the de-ephemeralize-at-source conventions rule: the null landed on disk ({"status":null}) and only an upsert replace dropped the key. Uninvestigated — root cause, shape, class (is field-deletion missing across the whole update/merge surface?), and prior-art are for the investigating agent.

evidence.reference: the triggering instance: update-block-item --match '{"id":"de-ephemeralize-at-source"}' --updates '{"status":null}' produced status:null on the item; upsert-block-item full-replace removed it

gaps.impact: A filed item cannot be cleaned of a stray field through the merge-update surface; the only route is a full-shape replacement, which re-states the whole item to remove one key.
gaps.proposed_resolution: To be determined by the investigation (Experience-Gap Handling): root cause in the update merge semantics, the class across the update/merge ops, and the canonical deletion affordance.

gaps.title: AJV validator cache keyed by unversioned $id — a long-lived process can validate against a stale compile when same-$id schema bodies evolve
gaps.description: validate (schema-validator.ts:111) reuses the shared AJV instance's compiled validator keyed by the schema's unversioned $id (pi-context://schemas/<name>). When one long-lived process compiles a different body under the same $id — e.g. a catalog body evolving across substrates, or a pinned target differing from a previously-compiled installed body — a later validate call runs against the stale compile, not the supplied body. Every supported flow is cache-coherent (the CLI is process-per-invocation; the canonical update/resolve loop compiles the same body the pin records); the stale case reproduces only by mutating same-$id bodies across substrates within one process. Surfaced by the TASK-051 adversarial probe; pre-existing, not introduced by the pending-blocked/resolve-blocked change. Class: $id-keyed compile caching vs evolving same-$id schema bodies — any consumer passing a schema OBJECT to validate can silently get the cached twin.

evidence.reference: validate compiles/reuses via the shared AJV instance keyed by the schema $id; the supplied body is ignored when a compile for that $id exists
the update/resolve flows that now pass pinned schema bodies to validate — the consumers the class could affect in a long-lived process

gaps.impact: In a single long-lived process validating different bodies under one $id, validation verdicts can reflect the first-compiled body — a wrong pass or wrong fail with no error. No supported CLI/op flow currently hits it; the exposure is future in-pi long-running consumers.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Key the compile cache by content (the schema's content hash or $id+version) or compile uncached when a body is supplied explicitly — determined by the investigating/implementing pass; verification: one process validating two different same-$id bodies returns body-correct verdicts for each.

gaps.title: A block whose array key holds a non-array value takes the no-items resync path and migrates without validation
gaps.description: A block file whose declared array key holds a non-array value (e.g. tasks: {}) is treated as having no items by the resync hasItems probe, so a catalog-ahead version bump resyncs it as migrated without validating the malformed content against the new schema — it never blocks, never marks. Uninvestigated — root cause confirmation, the class across the resync/validate surface, and prior-art are for the investigating agent.

evidence.reference: the resyncSchema hasItems probe (forEachBlockArray over the parsed block) yields no items for a non-array value at the array key; the no-items arm returns migrated without running validate

gaps.impact: A malformed block silently passes a version bump unvalidated; the blocked/marker diagnostic surface never engages for it.
gaps.proposed_resolution: To be determined by the investigation (Experience-Gap Handling): whether the no-items arm must distinguish absent/empty arrays from malformed non-array content, and the class across every consumer of the hasItems probe.

gaps.title: The install ceremony has no reflected CLI op — a CLI-driven bootstrap reaches a config-complete-but-unmaterialized substrate with no CLI op to advance it
gaps.description: installContext is surfaced to the operator only as the /context install slash-command handler and is listed in INTENTIONALLY_UNEXPOSED_WRITERS, so the reflecting CLI has no context-install op while its five lifecycle siblings (context-init / context-accept-all / context-switch / context-list / context-archive) are reflected ops. A CLI-only operator runs context-init + context-accept-all to a config-complete substrate whose schemas/ is empty and whose block files do not exist, with no CLI op to materialize them. Install is reachable ONLY via an interactive pi session's slash command: pi -p "/context install" does NOT dispatch it — prompt-mode hands the text to an agent, and install is not a registered op/tool, so no agent can call it either. There is no non-interactive path to install. Class: substrate-lifecycle operations unevenly reflected to the CLI — the CLI-parity family FGAP-030 named (FGAP-021..026 + FGAP-030); install is the last unreflected member of the init -> accept-all -> install bootstrap triad, so the CLI bootstrap is structurally incompletable on its own surface. The INTENTIONALLY_UNEXPOSED_WRITERS rationale (install is operator-facing via the command handler, not the op-registry) is the assertion to revisit; FGAP-030 set the precedent that a command-only surface unreachable from the CLI is a gap resolved by adding the reflected op.

evidence.reference: installContext(cwd, {overwrite}) — the install engine: copies installed_schemas[] bodies into <substrate>/schemas/, installed_blocks[] starters into <substrate>/<name>.json, base-stamps install baselines
the install entry of CONTEXT_SUBCOMMANDS — installContext is surfaced ONLY as the /context install slash-command handler
installContext listed in INTENTIONALLY_UNEXPOSED_WRITERS with the rationale that it is operator-facing via the command handler, not the op-registry — the stale assertion this gap revisits
the full investigation: root cause, the init+accept-all-then-empty-schemas/ repro, the pi -p seam, the FGAP-030 precedent, the class verdict

gaps.impact: A CLI-driven or agent-driven (non-pi) consumer cannot bootstrap a substrate to a usable state: init + accept-all leave schemas/ empty and no block files, and a subsequent append-block-item --autoId throws 'nextId: schema not found' (block-api.ts:2311). The capability is reachable only via an interactive pi session's slash command — no scriptable / non-interactive path exists (pi -p prompt-mode does not slash-dispatch, and install is not a registered tool an agent could call).
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: CLI bootstrap completability / lifecycle-op reflection parity
gaps.proposed_resolution: Determined by the implementing pass (Experience-Gap Handling): reflect install as a context-install OpDefinition like its lifecycle siblings (writer-classified via the DEC-0006 ctx/writer channel that context-init/context-accept-all already carry), revisiting the INTENTIONALLY_UNEXPOSED_WRITERS entry. Verification: a pure-CLI context-init -> context-accept-all -> context-install bootstrap materializes schemas + block files with no pi -p subprocess. Grounding: analysis/2026-06-13-install-surface-and-guard-scope-gaps.md.
gaps.closed_by: VER-045 / TASK-059 — the reflected context-install op completes the CLI bootstrap triad (init -> accept-all -> install); installContext is op-backed (removed from INTENTIONALLY_UNEXPOSED_WRITERS) with no behavior fork; a pure-CLI bootstrap materializes schemas + blocks + baseline and a subsequent append succeeds.

gaps.title: PreToolUse enforcement hooks scope on op-shape + block-name, never on the target substrate — they fire on writes to throwaway/non-active substrates outside the convention they protect
gaps.description: gap-register-guard.sh matches on write-op name (append/update/upsert-block-item) AND planning-block name/array-key, and block-pi-context-glue.sh matches on the pi-context CLI invocation shape; neither inspects --cwd or the active-substrate pointer (.pi-context.json contextDir). A planning-block write or a CLI invocation whose --cwd targets a throwaway/non-active substrate is blocked identically to one against the active substrate, though the filing-provenance and direct-drive conventions the hooks enforce scope to the active substrate (filings composed verbatim downstream INTO the active substrate; direct-drive discipline against the live substrate). The hook input carries the --cwd text in tool_input.command but the hooks do not parse it. Class: enforcement hooks scoped by op-shape rather than target-substrate, firing outside the active substrate they protect — covers both installed Bash-matcher hooks (gap-register-guard.sh, block-pi-context-glue.sh). The not-installed block-substrate-cli-bypass.sh spec (analysis/2026-06-06-substrate-cli-bypass-prevention-hook-spec.md) is the resource-scoped counter-example, matching the .context* family by path.

evidence.reference: the sole match test: (write-op name) AND (planning block-name or array-key); never reads --cwd or resolves the active-substrate pointer
the confirmed sibling: matches the pi-context CLI invocation shape alone; its block tests inspect only command-string glue, never --cwd or the active substrate

the investigation: matching logic quoted, the --cwd /tmp/gapAtest block repro, the block-pi-context-glue sibling confirmation, the block-substrate-cli-bypass.sh resource-scoped counter-example, the class verdict

gaps.impact: Every runtime demo or test that must seed a planning block into a throwaway --cwd substrate, or drive the CLI against one, hits the active-substrate guards — blocking legitimate non-active-substrate work. It blocked the TASK-033 runtime-demo seed against /tmp.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Determined by the implementing pass (Experience-Gap Handling): scope the hooks to the active substrate — parse --cwd from the command string (default to the project cwd when absent), resolve .pi-context.json contextDir at that cwd, and guard only writes/invocations targeting the active substrate; or a coarser approximation (guard only when --cwd is absent or names a .context* dir). The not-installed block-substrate-cli-bypass.sh spec's resource-scoped recognizer is the available pattern. Subtlety to honor: the hook input carries the --cwd text but no structured active-substrate resolution. Grounding: analysis/2026-06-13-install-surface-and-guard-scope-gaps.md.

gaps.title: relation_type registry coverage is incomplete and edge direction is unenforced: a decision raising a gap, a decision gated by an item, and a gap related to a gap are all unexpressible, and raise-edges are mistyped as the resolve-direction decision_addresses_gap
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: relation_type registry coverage completeness + write-time edge-direction enforcement
gaps.description: The relation_type registry's coverage is incomplete and edge direction is unenforced. Three warranted inter-item relations are unexpressible. (A) A decision RAISING/SPAWNING a framework-gap: the only decision->framework-gaps types are decision_addresses_gap and decision_escalates_underdetermined, both category data_flow and both resolve/escalate direction (the decision disposes of the gap); no raise-direction type exists. (B) A decision GATED BY / DEPENDING ON another item for enactment: decisions is the source_kind of no ordering relation_type except decision_supersedes_decision; features/stories/tasks each have *_gated_by_item and *_depends_on_item, decisions have no analog. (C) A framework-gap related to another framework-gap: the only framework-gaps-source types are gap_addressed_by_decision, gap_addressed_by_feature, gap_relates_to_issue, none targeting framework-gaps, so sibling/related gaps cannot be cross-referenced by a typed edge. relation_types entries carry only canonical_id/display_name/category/source_kinds/target_kinds with no description field, so an edge's direction is conveyed by the type name alone and no write-time check validates a written edge's direction and kinds against the type definition. Consequence: a decision that raises a gap and whose enactment depends on that gap's resolution (DEC-0018 -> FGAP-076) is filed as decision_addresses_gap, which a consumer reads as the decision resolving the gap (the inverse), and this gap itself cannot be typed-linked to its sibling gaps FGAP-007/FGAP-061. Root cause: the registry grew resolve-first and kind-by-kind, so relation coverage is partial and direction is unchecked; DEC-0018 is the first decision to raise-and-depend-on a gap, exposing the omissions together.

gaps.impact: A gate-aware deriver (FGAP-061) cannot compute a decision's open-because-blocked-on-gap state: the only decision->gap edge is data_flow (inert to ordering/readiness derivation), and consuming decision_addresses_gap as a gate would read the dependency backwards (treat the gap as resolved by the decision rather than blocking it). Decision enactment-dependencies are invisible to status/readiness derivation. Sibling and related gaps cannot be typed-linked, fragmenting the gap graph and pushing those cross-references into prose. Raise-vs-resolve and other misdirected edges accumulate substrate-wide with no write-time guard to catch them.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add three relation_types: a data_flow decision->framework-gaps raise type (decision_raises_gap); a decisions-source ordering gate type (decision_gated_by_item, target *) that FGAP-061's deriver consumes; and a framework-gaps->framework-gaps relation type (gap_relates_to_gap) for sibling/related-gap cross-reference. Retype the DEC-0018->FGAP-076 edge from decision_addresses_gap to decision_raises_gap, add a decision_gated_by_item edge from DEC-0018 to FGAP-076, and add gap_relates_to_gap edges from this gap to FGAP-007 and FGAP-061. Add a write-time check validating a written edge's source/target kinds and category against the relation_type definition so a misdirected edge is rejected at write. gaps.title: no forcing function for warranted non-invariant closure-table edges: edge presence is enforced only for requires-edge-backed relation_types, so warranted task_governed_by_decision / task_addresses_gap / research_informs_item edges are omitted with nothing to flag the absence
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: forcing-function coverage for warranted non-invariant closure-table edges
gaps.description: Closure-table edge presence is enforced only for relation_types backed by a requires-edge invariant: completed-task-has-verification, decision-cites-forcing-artifact, the three *-articulates-convention invariants, and decision-shows-derivation. The vocabulary-free requires-edge loop (the mechanism DEC-0016 establishes, context-sdk.ts:2299-2322) flags at context-validate any item in the invariant's block missing an edge of the named relation_types. Every other warranted relation_type — task_governed_by_decision, task_addresses_gap, task_addresses_feature, research_informs_item, the data_flow links — has no requires-edge invariant and is filed only by a manual append-relation step that runs after the item write; no surface flags a warranted-but-absent non-invariant edge. The forcing-function absence is both-surface: write-time has no completeness check tying an item's filed body to the edges its text entails, and context-validate has no rule flagging a missing non-invariant edge. Warranted means entailed by an item's filed text or by the relation_type's intended population — not universal (not every task addresses a gap), so a blanket requires-edge invariant is not the answer for every type. Edges an item's own text entails are written inconsistently or omitted, concentrated in pre-discipline history. General class: the five TASK->decision/gap edges missing at filing (TASK-010->DEC-0008 and ->FGAP-012, TASK-012->DEC-0009 and ->FGAP-015, TASK-014->FGAP-014) were the triggering instance; they have since been backfilled MANUALLY with no invariant added — the unforced-omission pattern the class predicts remains. FGAP-071 (gap->arc binding) is a contained sibling instance already filed narrowly.

gaps.impact: The closure-table graph is systematically incomplete for non-invariant relations: task_governed_by_decision, task_addresses_gap, research_informs_item are inconsistently populated. Any consumer reading the graph for provenance or derivation (gate-aware derivation FGAP-061, lineage walks, 'what addressed this gap') gets a partial answer, and the absences are invisible — no flag — so they accumulate silently as item counts grow; the flat-list-drift pattern FGAP-071 names for gaps, generalized to all warranted edges.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Generalize the TASK-041 / DEC-0016 pattern to the under-filed warranted relation_types. Per relation_type: where presence is universally entailed (a completed task that closed a gap; the enacting task of an enacted decision), add a requires-edge invariant making the edge unfileable-without, gated behind a backfill (the TASK-041 backfill-then-raise template, severity warning->error after backfill); where presence is conditional (not every task addresses a gap), add a validator check that flags an item whose body text names a closure or enactment ('closes FGAP-...', 'per DEC-...') without the corresponding edge — a text-entailment check rather than a blanket requirement. Whether any rule needs the FGAP-082 aggregate-over-edges invariant class is part of the mechanism choice. Relate to TASK-041 (per-type precedent), DEC-0016 (mechanism), FGAP-071 (contained instance), FGAP-082 (engine axis); the precise relates/informed-by relation types for gap->task, gap->decision, and gap->gap do not exist (intersects FGAP-090) and gap_addressed_by_decision would mistype the link as a resolution, so no typed relation edge to those relatives is written until those types land.

gaps.title: pi-context update's per-component write boundary (DEC-0018) is undocumented on the update surface — a blocked run's config mutation is contractual but nowhere stated
gaps.description: update applies per-component by decision: DEC-0018 (enacted, decision_addresses_gap -> this gap) extends DEC-0017's sibling-schema independence to the registry-propagation seam — a blocked schema rolls back byte-exact (resyncSchema, index.ts:1023-1203) while the additive config-registry propagation (index.ts:2150-2169) writes regardless of the schema-loop outcome. The mechanism is decided and correct; the residual gap is that no surface DOCUMENTS the contract: the update op description, the CLI text, and the README state neither that the boundary is per-component nor that a blocked run still lands the additive vocabulary. Outcome LEGIBILITY (the result object tying blocked to registryAdditions) is FGAP-076's scope, not this gap's.

R-0012 — live reproduction of the behavior the contract must document: a schema returned blocked while registryAdditions were written in the same run.

gaps.impact: Operators and agents reading the update surface have no stated contract for what a blocked run does to config.json; the decided per-component behavior reads as an accident instead of a boundary.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Document the per-component contract on the update surface: the context-update op description, the CLI text, and the README state the DEC-0018 boundary (blocked schema rolls back byte-exact; additive registry propagation and sibling schemas apply independently). Result-object legibility is FGAP-076.
