gaps.title: Write-time edge guard accepts an edge with a non-existent (dangling) or unregistered-alias endpoint, or one closing a cycle: only relation_type registration and present-endpoint source/target kind are rejected at write, while endpoint-existence and cycle remain validate-only — so a write-accepted edge is not always validate-clean
gaps.description: The write-time edge gate validateEdgeAgainstRegistry (packages/pi-context/src/context-sdk.ts:1568-1601), invoked by appendRelationByRef (1666) and appendRelationsByRef via assertEdgeValidForWrite (1629-1636), checks only (a) relation_type registration (1577-1583) and (b) presence-gated source/target-kind membership (1586-1599) — the kind check is conditioned on the endpoint resolving to a loc. A non-existent endpoint resolves to no loc (resolver status dangling), so the kind check is skipped and the edge is accepted and persisted; the helper never checks endpoint existence. The same resolution-gating skips an <alias>:<id> endpoint whose alias is unregistered (status unregistered). Cycle is not checked at write at all (the appendRelationsByRef JSDoc, 1788-1790, names endpoint resolution / cycle checks deferred to validateContext). validateContext's endpoint loop catches these only post-hoc: edge_endpoint_dangling (2204/2221), edge_endpoint_unregistered (2196/2213), and edge_cycle_detected merged from validateRelations (2279). This is the endpoint-existence residual of FGAP-090/TASK-062, which achieved write-validate parity on the registration + kind axes but left endpoint-existence and cycle validate-only. General class: the write-time edge guard rejects a strict subset of what validateContext rejects; full parity requires it to additionally reject dangling endpoints, unregistered-alias endpoints, and cycles, so a write-accepted edge is always validate-clean.

evidence.reference: validateEdgeAgainstRegistry gates source/target-kind membership on parentLoc/childLoc being truthy; an endpoint that resolves to no loc (dangling/unregistered) skips the check and the helper returns no error — no endpoint-existence check exists

helper JSDoc: a lens_bin / dangling / unregistered endpoint carries no loc and is skipped for the kind check (endpoint-resolution failures are validateContext's own surface) — documents the gap as intended
appendRelationsByRef JSDoc: relation_type registration / endpoint resolution / cycle checks deferred to validateContext — names endpoint-resolution + cycle as the write-deferred checks
validateContext emits edge_endpoint_dangling (error) for a parent/child that does not resolve to any item — the post-hoc catch for the write-accepted edge
validateContext emits edge_endpoint_unregistered (error) for an endpoint naming an unregistered substrate alias — the sibling resolution-failure also write-accepted
validateContext merges edge_cycle_detected from validateRelations — the cycle check absent at write
test appends FGAP-1 --gap_relates_to_gap--> FGAP-2 with both endpoints dangling and asserts appended === true — encodes the current write-accept of dangling endpoints; a fix changes this test
the triggering instance: appendRelationByRef(NOPE-999 --decision_raises_gap--> FGAP-001) ACCEPTED and persisted; validateContext later flags edge_endpoint_dangling

empirical repro on a fresh /tmp adopt-only substrate: appended=true, ON_DISK_COUNT=1, validateContext two edge_endpoint_dangling errors; root cause + class + prior-art (FGAP-090 closed-adjacent, FGAP-007/TASK-027 sibling-direction, FGAP-091 inverse-absence) — untracked

gaps.impact: A relation can be written and committed naming an endpoint that does not exist (a typo'd or premature id) or an unregistered cross-substrate alias, or closing a cycle, with no write-time signal — the porcelain returns appended: true. The defect surfaces only at a later context-validate pass (if one runs), divorced in time from the write that introduced it, so a CLI/op caller filing an edge gets no immediate feedback that the edge is broken. This is the precise inverse of the TASK-062-enforced kind check: a present-but-wrong-kind endpoint is rejected at write, but an absent endpoint (strictly more broken) is accepted — write-time rejection coverage is non-monotonic in edge brokenness. Broken edges accumulate substrate-wide between validate runs, and --dryRun previews (which run the same gate, 1666-1675) also fail to predict the dangling-edge defect.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend the write-time edge guard to full write-validate parity — reject at write everything validateContext rejects for an edge: the residual endpoint-existence (edge_endpoint_dangling), unregistered-alias (edge_endpoint_unregistered), and cycle (edge_cycle_detected) checks, in addition to the registration + present-endpoint-kind checks TASK-062 already hoisted. Mechanism: the write-time resolver already classifies each endpoint status (the same resolveRef validateContext uses, built by buildWriteTimeEdgeValidator, 1612-1621) — surface a dangling/unregistered status as a write-time rejection rather than a skip; add a prospective-cycle check against the existing edges (mirroring the validateRelations cycle pass). Reconcile structured-endpoints.test.ts:288-310 (currently asserts dangling-append succeeds) as part of the change — whether a legitimately forward-referencing append needs an explicit opt-out, or dangling is always a write error, is a genuine open implementation choice for the closing task. Pairs with FGAP-007/TASK-027 (sibling write-time edge-direction guard); the endpoint-existence residual of the closed FGAP-090.

gaps.title: packaged catalog relation_types is a strict subset of the live config — catalog↔config vocabulary drift with no parity forcing-function
gaps.description: The packaged catalog `packages/pi-context/samples/conception.json` `relation_types[]` (34 entries) is a strict subset of the live `.context/config.json` `relation_types[]` (40) — every catalog relation_type is in live config, zero catalog-only. 6 relation_types are live-only: `task_gated_by_item`, `session_touches_item`, `task_advances_story`, `feature_advances_story`, `decision_derived_from_item`, `decision_escalates_underdetermined` — each hand-added to live config during substrate work (for an edge / lens / invariant) and never back-ported to the catalog. No forcing-function (build gate / parity check) ensures the catalog carries the relation_types the shipped library/derivations consume, and none records which live-only vocabulary is intentionally substrate-local vs catalog-worthy, so drift is silent. `task_gated_by_item` is the triggering instance: a fresh install-ceremony substrate (context-init → accept-all → install) lacks it, so FGAP-061's gate-aware `currentState` (which consumes it) has nothing to derive AND a `task_gated_by_item` edge is rejected at write — `validateEdgeAgainstRegistry` (context-sdk.ts:1496-1502) is registration-first and short-circuits, `assertEdgeValidForWrite` (context-sdk.ts:1548) throws — so task gating is unwritable on a fresh substrate while feature/story/decision gating ships. The other 5 are latent (substrate-organizational), the same drift.

evidence.reference: 34 relation_types; task_gated_by_item absent (git log -S finds zero commits adding it to this file); the three sibling *_gated_by_item present since authoring commit b8a9d7e

40 relation_types; strict superset of the catalog by 6 entries; task_gated_by_item added by commit 669890c (2026-06-07) and never back-ported; the FGAP-090 back-port a582939 was scoped to its three relations only

validateEdgeAgainstRegistry registration-first short-circuit + assertEdgeValidForWrite throw + appendRelationByRef gate — an unregistered task_gated_by_item edge is rejected at write time on a fresh substrate (unwritable, not merely inert)

gaps.impact: On every catalog-built substrate the gated_by pattern is incomplete at the task altitude (the most common gating level): a task gate is unwritable (hard write rejection) and gate-aware readiness is inert, while feature/story/decision gates ship. The 5 non-triggering live-only relations are latent debt that surfaces the same way as each is consumed. Filing only the task_gated_by_item symptom leaves the class unaddressed and invites duplicate sibling filings.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: registry parity / drift detection (catalog ⊇ consumed vocabulary)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Superseded by FGAP-102 (multi-registry catalog<->config parity + state_derivation referential integrity; the relation_types-only framing here is one of >=5 dimensions). Original narrow proposal: NOW (symptom + class backfill): decide which of the 6 live-only relation_types are catalog-worthy vs intentionally substrate-local, and back-port the catalog-worthy set into samples/conception.json relation_types[] (at minimum task_gated_by_item, the relation a shipped derivation consumes). FORWARD (the class fix): add a build-time parity forcing-function asserting the catalog superset of the relation_types the shipped library/derivations consume -- mirroring FGAP-010's scripts/parity-check.ts structural answer for the code-surface trio, here for the catalog<->config vocabulary surface -- with an explicit record distinguishing intentionally-substrate-local from catalog-worthy vocabulary, so drift cannot recur silently.

gaps.title: Config-schema evolution has no load-time migration path
gaps.description: loadConfigForDir parses config.json then calls validateFromFile(bundledSchemaPath("config"), …) and returns — no schema_version check, no migration step. A schema-name-keyed migration mechanism exists (validateBlockWithMigrationForDir: compare schema.version to data.schema_version, walk the registered chain on mismatch, then validate) and "config" is a valid schemaName, but config-load never invokes it. When config.schema.json advances to a shape the stored config does not satisfy, every config-loading op fails validation (exit 5) with no path to carry the config across the version gap. The class: read-time validation and migration require a versioned-data envelope; config carries no schema_version, so the mechanism is uninvoked and config's raw-validate brick is the acute symptom.

evidence.reference: loadConfigForDir parses config.json then validateFromFile(bundledSchemaPath("config"), data, …) and returns — no schema_version check, no migration call

validateBlockWithMigrationForDir: block-data load reads schema.version vs the item's schema_version, runs runMigrations over the registered chain on mismatch, then validates — the mechanism config-load lacks
the migration registry is keyed by schemaName; resolve()/runMigrations would walk a "config" chain identically to a block name; config-load simply never calls it

gaps.impact: A breaking config-schema bump strands the live substrate: config.json fails validation against the rebuilt CLI and every config-loading op exits 5, with no sanctioned recovery (amend-config also validates-before-mutate). Reproduced in this project: the TASK-020 state_derivation.blocked_by shape change bricked the live .context until the change was reverted. Until a config-migration path exists, every future config-schema shape change carries the same live-substrate-bricking risk.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: schema migration (forward migration / version-gated transform applied at load)
gaps.proposed_resolution: RESOLVED at TASK-070: loadConfigForDir migrates-then-validates via the schema-name-keyed config chain (fail-fast on unresolvable chain, no raw-validate fallback); map_each declarative-transform primitive shipped (table + set-on-each modes); catalog identity config 1.0.0→1.7.0 declaration in samples/migrations.json with idempotent seeding at every ceremony entry point (write ceremonies materialize-then-seed; read ceremonies no-op on a nonexistent dir); the live substrate's config chain filed via write-schema-migration. Verified VER-060. --- Original: Add a load-time config-migration path mirroring validateBlockWithMigrationForDir: loadConfigForDir reads the config's schema_version and, on mismatch with the bundled schema version, walks the schema-name-keyed migration chain for "config" before validating. Requires a declarative-transform primitive able to map array elements (map_each) and a config migration registry entry. Verified design + corrected wiring: analysis/2026-06-18-config-migration-corrected-implementation-steps.md (and the same-date feasibility/validation md's). File at the class level (schema-evolution safety: read-time validation + migration require a versioned-data envelope).

gaps.title: Config repair is impossible once the stored config is invalid (validate-before-mutate)
gaps.description: amendConfigEntryForDir loads the existing config (which validates it) before applying any mutation — only a dryRun option, no force/skip — so an already-invalid config cannot be repaired through the CLI: the write path refuses on the pre-existing invalid state before the corrective mutation runs. accept-all is not a recovery path either — it never-clobbers a populated config. Independent of the missing load-time migration (FGAP-095): a config whose schema_version equals the current schema version (no migration applies) but whose body is invalid (corruption, hand-edit, or a schema change with no registered migration) throws at BOTH loadConfigForDir and amendConfigEntryForDir — unmigratable AND unrepairable. Migrate-on-load resolves only the version-gap subset of the deadlock.

evidence.reference: amendConfigEntryForDir loads (validates) the config before mutating; only a dryRun option, no force/skip — an already-invalid config throws on load before the corrective write
accept-all (adoptConception) never-clobbers a populated config, so it is not a recovery path for an invalid config
loadConfigForDir validates the raw config on every load, so any read of an invalid config also throws — the read path and the repair path share the same pre-validation

gaps.impact: Once the stored config is invalid for any reason not covered by a registered migration, there is no sanctioned CLI path back to validity — the only recourse is the forbidden direct-edit of .context/config.json. This is the recovery-side root cause of the incident's unrecoverability: even setting aside the missing migration, validate-before-mutate means the CLI cannot dig the substrate out of an invalid state. Proven independent of FGAP-095 in the /tmp repro (a no-version-delta invalid config, both unmigratable and unrepairable).
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: recovery / repair path (validate-after-mutate, or a force-repair write that validates the result rather than the pre-existing state)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Provide a sanctioned recovery path that does not refuse on a pre-existing invalid config: a repair/force write (or a recover op) that applies the corrective mutation and validates the RESULT, rather than validating the stored config on load and throwing before the repair can be applied. Scope is distinct from the load-time migration (FGAP-095): migration carries a version-gap config forward; this restores any invalid config regardless of version. Sibling of FGAP-095 in the config-schema-lifecycle root-cause set.

gaps.title: No additive / expand-contract discipline for config-schema changes
gaps.description: Nothing prevents or flags a breaking (non-additive) config-schema shape change — a key removal/rename, or a new required field — before it ships. The config schema root is additionalProperties:false, so the moment a key is removed/renamed the existing config carries a now-unknown property and fails validation; a new required field strands a config that lacks it. This is an authoring/build-time gap, distinct from the runtime gaps FGAP-095 (no load-time migration) and FGAP-096 (no recovery path): even with migration and recovery in place, the absence of an additive discipline means every breaking change requires a migration rather than avoiding the break, and no gate catches a breaking config-schema diff before release. Distinct from FGAP-094, which is vocabulary/catalog-superset-config drift, not breaking-key discipline.

evidence.reference: root required:["schema_version","block_kinds"] + additionalProperties:false — a removed/renamed key or a new required field invalidates an existing config on the next load
no breaking-diff gate exists on config-schema edits: nothing in the build/release path compares an old->new config.schema.json shape and flags a non-additive change

gaps.impact: A breaking config-schema change can ship unflagged; combined with FGAP-095/096 it bricks the live substrate (the incident this set documents). An additive/expand-contract discipline would make most config bumps non-bricking by construction, independent of migration code — the cheapest structural mitigation of the brick class.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: expand-contract / parallel change (additive, backward-compatible schema evolution)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Adopt an additive/expand-contract discipline for config-schema evolution — new fields land optional; existing fields are not removed/renamed until a later contract release — enforced by a build-time breaking-diff gate on config.schema.json that flags a non-additive change absent a paired migration. Pairs with FGAP-095 (load-time migration) and FGAP-096 (recovery path); build-time-gate family with FGAP-094.
gaps.closed_by: TASK-072 / VER-064 — the expand-contract discipline is enforced mechanically by the build-time breaking-diff gate scripts/check-config-schema.ts (pre-commit 5th line + pull_request CI step, staged and --base modes diffing against the prior git revision): a removed/renamed property key anywhere in the config.schema.json properties tree, or a new required entry on a pre-existing object, fails the gate unless the same change set advances the schema version AND declares a packaged config migration reaching that version in samples/migrations.json; additive changes (new optional fields, description/enum additions, a newly-added optional object's initial requireds) pass. Live-demonstrated: a staged removal of properties.lenses failed naming the JSON path; the same removal paired with a version bump + packaged decl passed; clean-tree runs pass in both modes. Merged b21089e8.

gaps.title: Issues are not a gap-sibling first-class open-work kind — a task cannot focus on / address an issue as it can a gap
gaps.description: Framework-gaps are first-class open work in the PM model: task_addresses_gap relates a task to a gap, the task-completed-gap-closed invariant enforces closure, and framework-gaps is a next_ranked open-work kind in state_derivation so currentState surfaces open gaps as focus / next-actions. Issues have no parity — there is no task_addresses_issue relation (issues are reached only via decision_addresses_issue / feature_resolves_issue / gap_relates_to_issue), no issue-closure invariant, and issues are not a next_ranked kind, so currentState never surfaces an open issue as focusable next work. User-verbatim requirement: "we need gaps and issues to be sibling class items throughout the substrate. a task can and must be able to focus as they can with gaps on issues." currentState (context-sdk.ts:733-961) and lens-view are already kind-general (FEAT-004 / TASK-020), so the asymmetry is purely in config/catalog vocabulary (relation_types, invariants, state_derivation) plus two additive issue-schema lifecycle fields — not in code.

evidence.reference: task_addresses_gap (tasks->framework-gaps) present; there is no task_addresses_issue — a task cannot address an issue the way it addresses a gap
task-completed-gap-closed present; no issue analog (e.g. task-completed-issue-resolved), so completing a task against an issue is not lifecycle-checked
framework-gaps is a next_ranked open-work kind; issues is absent, so currentState never ranks/focuses an open issue
currentState is kind-general (reads state_derivation); confirms the asymmetry is config/catalog vocabulary, not deriver code
issues lacks resolved_at and an x-lifecycle block that framework-gaps has — the schema-side lifecycle-parity delta
the gap-explore investigation: the full five-axis asymmetry map, the required-vs-acceptable-divergence determination, and the prior-art search (genuinely unfiled)

gaps.impact: Issues are filed but invisible to the derived focus / what's-next surface and not closeable-by-task in the tracked model — they are second-class relative to gaps. issue-004 (and every issue) sits outside the task-driven close loop that gaps enjoy: no task can focus on or address it, and its resolution is not lifecycle-checked. Sibling parity is required for issues to participate in derivation, focus, and task-closure exactly as gaps do.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: first-class sibling block-kind / open-work parity (kind-general PM derivation + relation/invariant parity)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Per the investigation, group the required set: (a) add relation_types task_addresses_issue + issue_relates_to_issue (the reverse issue_addressed_by_* and the decision_raises_issue / missing-convention / escalation analogs are NOT required — gaps are the escalation sink by design, an acceptable divergence); (b) add invariant task-completed-issue-resolved (mirror of task-completed-gap-closed, severity warning); (c) add a state_derivation.next_ranked issues entry (bucket todo, rank_field priority, rank_order critical->low) — no deriver code change, since issues.status open already buckets to todo; (d) two additive issue-schema fields resolved_at + an x-lifecycle block; (e) issues-by-status + issues-by-priority lenses, and catalog back-port (samples/conception.json) of every new relation_type / invariant / lens. The catalog back-port leg interacts with FGAP-094: a new live relation_type absent from the packaged catalog makes a fresh accept-all substrate reject its edge at write. Status-label / priority-scale / category-facet divergences between the two schemas are acceptable kind-specific differences and are NOT force-symmetrized.

gaps.title: Block data carries no schema_version — read-time validation and migration are inert for all blocks
gaps.description: Block data files carry no schema_version: the write path (writeTypedFile / writeBlockForDir) stamps only author/identity fields and never adds one, and no block schema defines a top-level schema_version (envelopes are additionalProperties:false, which rejects it). Version lives only in the schema file. With schema_version absent from every block's data, two safety mechanisms are inert for all blocks: the read-time AJV hook (gated on schema_version presence, block-api.ts:785-789) never fires, and the version-mismatch branch of validateBlockWithMigrationForDir (schema-validator.ts:225-239) never triggers. Block schema-evolution safety runs only via the explicit install/update path (baseline content_hash-tracked), never on read. The class: read-time validation and migration are inert whenever the versioned-data envelope is absent.

evidence.reference: writeTypedFile / writeBlockForDir stamp author/identity fields only; schema_version is carried through only if the incoming data already has it — nothing in the write path produces one
representative block schema: declares only the array key as a top-level property under additionalProperties:false; the version lives in the schema file's version field, not in the data envelope
the read-time AJV hook is gated on schema_version being present in the block data; with it absent the hook never fires
validateBlockWithMigrationForDir's version-mismatch branch compares schema.version to data.schema_version; with data.schema_version absent it never triggers

gaps.impact: Block data is validated at write (whole-block AJV) and by explicit context-validate, but never on read and never migrated on a version gap, because schema_version is absent. A schema-evolution change is invisible until the next write to that block, and a catalog-declared version-bump migration never applies to existing block data on read. Block schema evolution has no read-time safety.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: versioned data envelope / schema_version stamping (read-time version-gated validation + forward migration)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Stamp the current schema version onto the block envelope (top-level schema_version) on the write path, and admit a single optional top-level schema_version property on each block schema envelope (a named relaxation of the envelope additionalProperties:false, not a wholesale opening; per-item objects are unchanged). The existing read-time AJV hook (block-api.ts:785-789) and the migration version-mismatch branch (schema-validator.ts:225-239) then become active for all blocks: on a version gap, a registered migration walks the whole block forward, and absent one the read/write gate throws. Per-item stamping is out of scope (per the block-api.ts:782-784 comment) — it is a distinct, larger task.
gaps.closed_by: TASK-073 / VER-063 — the filed resolution shipped verbatim: every catalog block schema envelope admits a single optional top-level schema_version (named relaxation only; per-item objects unchanged), and the write path (writeTypedFile, generically, self-gated on the schema declaring the property + carrying a version) stamps the envelope to the schema's current version on every block write. The read-time AJV hook and the version-mismatch migration branch are thereby active for all blocks: a version gap walks the registered chain forward on read and write, and an unresolvable gap fails loud (read throws; write refused, block file byte-unchanged). Live on the active substrate: installed schemas resynced via update, tasks.json/verification.json envelopes stamped 1.0.1 by the closure writes, stamped reads routing through the hook. Per-item stamping remains out of scope as filed.

gaps.title: A sub-element of a substrate item has no identity — meaningful parts held in a nested array cannot be referenced, related, verified, or tracked across content edits
gaps.description: Items carry ids; the parts inside an item — entries in a nested array — do not. Nothing can point at one such part, bind to it, or recognize it as the same part after its content changes; its only handle is array position (moves on insert/delete/reorder) or prose (changes on rewording). Acute instance: a task's acceptance_criteria is a plain string array (no per-criterion id), and verification.criteria_results[] keys each result on the criterion's text ({criterion:string,status,evidence?}), so the sole criterion-to-result join is exact prose match. Class: any meaningful nested-array part lacking identity (evidence entries, options_considered, consequences, criteria_results), not the criteria instance alone.

evidence.reference: findNestedIdBearingArrays flags only nested arrays whose items already carry an id (promote to a top-level entity + membership edge); acceptance_criteria and criteria_results carry no id, so the check does not cover them
acceptance_criteria is an array of string with no per-criterion id (CLI-verified 2026-06-19)
criteria_results.items = {criterion:string, status, evidence?} — the per-criterion result is keyed on the criterion's text, no id (CLI-verified 2026-06-19)

gaps.impact: Per-criterion verification is not durable across the iterate-to-zero edits the canonical process requires: when a criterion's text changes, its criteria_results entry can no longer be matched to it, because nothing establishes same-criterion identity across the edit. Generally, no nested-array part can be the stable target of a reference, relation, or verification across content edits.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Resolution not yet determined; to be filed as a separate decision.

gaps.title: validateRelations conflates content relations with lens-axis relations: a hand-curated edge-materialization lens (derived_from_field null, bins []) over a content relation_type yields spurious edge_parent_not_in_bins on every real advancer edge, forcing context-validate-relations to invalid
gaps.description: validateRelations (context.ts:1712-1906) maps EVERY config.lenses[] entry by its relation_type with no filter (1720-1724) and, for any edge whose relation_type matches a lens, unconditionally applies the binning contract: edge.parent must be a declared lens bin (1771-1783). It has no representation for an edge-materialization lens — a hand-curated lens (derived_from_field: null) declared only so context-edges-for-lens can materialize its edges, whose parents are an open item-id set, not a fixed bin-label set. The two story-advancer lenses are exactly this: story-advancers (relation_type task_advances_story) and story-advancers-features (relation_type feature_advances_story), both kind:target, derived_from_field:null, bins:[]. Their relation_types also carry the real content advancer edges (TASK->STORY, FEAT->STORY). For each such content edge the structured item parent (e.g. TASK-046) is not a bin label, [].includes(...) is false, and an edge_parent_not_in_bins error fires (an errorCodes member), so context-validate-relations returns invalid on structurally-correct edges (29 live: 13 task_advances_story + 14 feature_advances_story). The lens author foresaw this — commit f2b8f91 states 'lens-view binning inapplicable (bins are edge-parent labels, an open task-id set)' — but the validator has no way to honor it. General class: a single relation_type overloaded as both a content relation and a lens projection axis, where validateRelations only models the projection axis.

evidence.reference: lensesByRelType maps EVERY config.lenses[] by relation_type with no kind/derived_from_field/bins filter — an edge-materialization lens is registered identically to a binning lens
the lens branch unconditionally requires edge.parent in lens.bins; a structured item parent falls back to its refname (1774-1776) and [].includes(refname) is false -> edge_parent_not_in_bins
edge_parent_not_in_bins is in errorCodes -> hasErrors -> status 'invalid' (not a warning)

lenses story-advancers (relation_type task_advances_story) and story-advancers-features (relation_type feature_advances_story): both kind:target, target:story, derived_from_field:null, bins:[] — read via pi-context read-config --registry lenses
relation_types task_advances_story / feature_advances_story: category data_flow, source_kinds tasks/features, target_kinds story — content relations, not lens-axis-only
derived_from_field:null is the hand-curated-edge lens marker ('hand-curated edges in relations.json'), confirming these lenses are edge-materialization, not status/priority binning

lens-declaring commit message: 'context-edges-for-lens --lensId story-advancers materializes every task->story criteria binding; lens-view binning inapplicable (bins are edge-parent labels, an open task-id set)' — the conflict acknowledged at authoring

gaps.impact: context-validate-relations returns invalid on a substrate whose edges are all structurally correct, so the integrity gate is unusable as a green/red signal: every real advancer edge added (each task/feature->story) increases the false count (29 live, was 1 on 2026-06-14). For ~6 days the invalid verdict has been routed around as a 'pre-existing backlog' in every session that ran the gate, masking any genuine relation defect that the same op would surface. Any consumer trusting context-validate-relations status (closure gate, CI, the canonical pipeline's integrity step) gets a false negative on the substrate's health. The pattern recurs for every hand-curated edge-materialization lens declared over a content relation_type.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: content-relation vs lens-axis-relation conflation in validateRelations (edge-materialization lens)
gaps.proposed_resolution: RESOLVED at TASK-076 / commit e1cf07f via mechanism (b): the lens parent-in-bins check now guards on parentBin !== null (endpointBin non-null), so a structured item parent never enters the bins check and an edge-materialization lens no longer false-invalidates content edges; a genuine lens_bin parent not in bins still errors. The 29 live advancer edges validate clean; a new edge-materialization-lens clean-validation test guards it. The explicit lens-flag/kind marker sketched in the original mechanism (b) proved unnecessary -- the endpointBin-null guard suffices. --- Original plan: Make validateRelations distinguish a content relation from a lens projection axis so an edge-materialization lens does not impose the parent-in-bins contract on content edges. Two candidate mechanisms (the closing task decides): (a) skip the parent-in-bins check for a lens with derived_from_field:null AND bins:[]; or (b) preferred for precision: only treat an edge as a lens-bin edge when its parent is structurally a lens_bin endpoint (endpointBin non-null). Relate to FGAP-093, FGAP-073, FGAP-082.
gaps.closed_by: human/davidryan@gmail.com -- fixed by TASK-076 (commit e1cf07f) via mechanism (b): validateRelations guards the lens parent-in-bins check on parentBin !== null (endpointBin non-null), so a structured item parent never enters the bins check while a genuine lens_bin parent not in bins still errors. Verified VER-059: full suite 0 failures (rewritten edge_parent_not_in_bins test + new edge-materialization-lens clean test) and live .context context-validate-relations now clean -- the 29 advancer false-invalids gone.

gaps.title: Packaged catalog drifts below live config across all four propagated registries and ships a state_derivation reference to an unregistered relation_type, with no forcing-function for vocabulary parity or state_derivation referential integrity
gaps.description: The packaged catalog `packages/pi-context/samples/conception.json` is a strict subset of the live `.context/config.json` across every keyed registry that `mergeCatalogRegistries` propagates (context.ts:1127), not relation_types alone: 6 live-only relation_types (`task_gated_by_item`, `session_touches_item`, `decision_derived_from_item`, `decision_escalates_underdetermined`, `task_advances_story`, `feature_advances_story`), 4 live-only lenses (`gaps-by-priority`, `features-by-status`, `story-advancers`, `story-advancers-features`), 1 live-only invariant (`decision-shows-derivation`), 1 live-only block_kind (`session-notes`); zero catalog-only entries. Each live-only entry is consumed by shipped code (the edge write-gate, lens-view, the invariant engine, currentState), so a fresh install-ceremony substrate (init -> accept-all -> install) lacks consumed vocabulary. The catalog is also internally inconsistent: `state_derivation.blocked_by.relation_types` lists `task_gated_by_item` (conception.json:503) that the catalog's own `relation_types[]` never registers, and `mergeCatalogRegistries` inherits the catalog `state_derivation` whole on a fresh substrate (context.ts:1150-1152), so every catalog-built substrate ships a blocker derivation referencing an unregistered relation while `task_gated_by_item` edges are write-rejected (`assertEdgeValidForWrite` throws, context-sdk.ts:1628-1635) -- task gating is both unwritable and unresolvable. No forcing-function asserts catalog >= the vocabulary shipped derivations consume across the propagated registries, and none checks that `state_derivation` references resolve against registered relation_types/block_kinds; both drift and dangling-reference recur silently. The relation_types-only axis (the retired FGAP-094 framing) is one of at least five dimensions.

evidence.reference: state_derivation.blocked_by.relation_types lists task_gated_by_item, the file's only occurrence; it is never registered in relation_types[] -- the catalog is internally inconsistent and a fresh accept-all ships a blocked_by reference to an unregistered relation

5 live-only relation_types grep-verified absent (0 occurrences each): session_touches_item, decision_derived_from_item, decision_escalates_underdetermined, task_advances_story, feature_advances_story; task_gated_by_item present only as a state_derivation reference

live-only lenses (gaps-by-priority, features-by-status, story-advancers, story-advancers-features), invariant (decision-shows-derivation), and block_kind (session-notes) grep-verified absent (0 occurrences each)

mergeCatalogRegistries propagates exactly relation_types/invariants/block_kinds/lenses (line 1127, additive append-only) and inherits the catalog state_derivation whole when the target lacks one (1150-1152); status_buckets/layers are not propagated

validateEdgeAgainstRegistry flags an unregistered relation_type and short-circuits; assertEdgeValidForWrite throws -- a task_gated_by_item edge is rejected at write time on a fresh catalog substrate. Consumption verified: write-gate (relation_types); validateProject 2298/2334 (invariants); currentState 784-794 reads sd.blocked_by.relation_types (state_derivation); context-lens-view (lenses)
investigation of record: per-registry empirical drift diff, consumption sites, propagation-vs-parity-scope map, and the FGAP-098 multi-registry back-port corroboration (commit 4376399)

gaps.impact: On every catalog-built substrate: consumed vocabulary is missing across four registries (relation_types, lenses, invariants, block_kinds), and the shipped state_derivation references a relation_type the substrate cannot register or write -- so gate-aware readiness has a dangling dependency and task-level gating is unwritable, while the integrity surface that should catch a state_derivation referencing unregistered vocabulary does not exist. Addressing only the relation_types axis (FGAP-094) leaves three drifting registries and the state_derivation referential defect unaddressed and invites duplicate sibling filings.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: registry parity + state_derivation referential integrity (catalog superset of every consumed config registry; state_derivation references resolve against registered relation_types/block_kinds)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires further investigation and determination.

gaps.title: PM lifecycle vocabularies admit no deliberate-suspension status on any kind; a paused arc is unrepresentable (phase acute: derived focus presents it as active)
gaps.description: No shipped lifecycle status enum on any of the 13 status-bearing block kinds carries a deliberate-suspension value ("paused by the user, to resume"): every kind expresses only active, blocked-by-something, or terminal states. The bucket layer beneath already reserves the value — STATUS_VOCABULARY_DEFAULTS maps paused -> blocked (status-vocab.ts:80) and every derivation consumes buckets — so only the per-kind schema enums (write-time AJV) reject it. Acute instance: phase, the only work-bearing kind with zero non-active non-terminal value AND the state_derivation focus_fallback kind — a user-paused phase filed in-progress becomes the derived focus the moment no task is in flight (focus_fallback: first phase bucketing in_progress, context-sdk.ts:941-958), presenting the suspended arc as the active work. Triggering instance: PHASE-PORT-OPS, declared paused by the user 2026-07-02, filed in-progress with no representable alternative. Scratchpad-duplicate reproduction: with in-flight empty, focus = "phase: PHASE-PORT-OPS (…)"; counterfactual status paused yields "no active focus." with zero deriver change.

evidence.reference: The phase status enum is exactly ["planned","in-progress","completed"] — no deliberate-suspension value. Installed copy identical at version 2.0.1 (read-schema --schemaName phase --path properties.phases.items.properties.status).
STATUS_VOCABULARY_DEFAULTS already maps paused -> blocked: the bucket layer reserves the value, but no shipped schema enum can emit it — write-time AJV rejects it on every kind.

focus_fallback consumption: with in_flight empty, the first phase bucketing in_progress becomes the derived focus string — a paused-but-filed-in-progress phase is promoted to THE active focus (state_derivation registry: focus_fallback {kind: phase, bucket: in_progress}).

Full investigation: per-kind class table (13 status-bearing kinds, none suspendable), scratchpad-duplicate reproduction (focus becomes 'phase: PHASE-PORT-OPS (…)' when nothing is in flight; counterfactual paused yields 'no active focus.' with zero deriver change), prior-art sweep (no existing coverage), resolution dimension set.

gaps.impact: Derived position/focus/status misrepresent a user-paused arc as active work: context-status counts it in-progress in every projection, and currentState's focus_fallback promotes it to THE derived focus whenever nothing is in flight. The substrate cannot record the true state of a suspended arc, so session-start derivation (the canonical source of position) is false until the arc resumes or completes.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Additive enum widening: add a deliberate-suspension value (paused) to the status enum, dual-surface per the FGAP-102 parity discipline (samples/schemas/phase.schema.json enum + its lifecycle prose AND the installed schema via /context update), schema version 2.0.1 -> 2.1.0. No deriver change: paused already buckets to blocked (status-vocab.ts:80), removing focus eligibility (observed); no state_derivation registry change; additive, so no data migration; the milestone rollup invariant behaves truthfully (a paused phase keeps its milestone unreached). Docs-surface-sync: status-vocab.ts block comment + schema description strings enumerate the lifecycle. Per-kind extent (phase only vs the class set — tasks/features/story approximate via blocked; issues/requirements deferred is won't-work-on shading) is a user scope determination at task time, surfaced by the class table in the findings document, not decided here.

gaps.title: SKILL.md generation executes extension factories against the live environment — shipped package docs are a function of local project state (crash → silently partial output; substrate-declared composites → embedded as package surface)
gaps.description: scripts/generate-skills.js derives each SKILL.md by executing the package's built dist factory against a mock pi in the INVOKING cwd, recording registrations. Two consequences: (1) a factory throw is caught with a warning ("expected for extensions needing runtime context"), registrations made before the throw are kept, and the generator exits 0 — truncated output is indistinguishable from complete output (realized 2026-07-02: pi-agent-dispatch's factory reads the live substrate config before its two pi.on registrations; a TASK-070 migration-window read threw; the SKILL.md lost its events section and was committed as complete, corrected at 6d91cb0). (2) The generator records ALL registrations with no origin distinction, and pi-agent-dispatch's factory registers substrate-declared composite tools (config.tool_operations[], FEAT-010 runtime design — correct for runtime, wrong as docs input): a project with tool_operations entries would embed its own configuration into the package's shipped documentation (dormant: live tool_operations is []). Class: package documentation must be a pure function of the package; the generator makes it a function of whatever substrate the generating machine holds.

evidence.reference: Factory executed against a mock pi in the invoking cwd; a throw is caught and normalized to a warning, registrations captured before the throw are kept, exit 0 — partial output shipped as complete.
<tools_reference> composition iterates ALL captured registrations with no distinction between package-defined and environment-derived tools.

Factory order: 6 registerTool calls, then loadComposites(process.cwd(), pi) (live substrate config read), then the two pi.on event registrations — a throw at the read truncates the events surface; substrate-declared composites register before the generator's capture.

loadComposites reads the active substrate's config.tool_operations[] and calls pi.registerTool per composite entry — intended FEAT-010 runtime design; becomes a docs-content source only via the generator.

Realized instance: commit 08f00f9 shipped the events-section-truncated output as complete; 6d91cb0 restored it. Git archaeology dated the three enabling conditions (generator catch 2026-03-18; factory-time substrate read 2026-05-28; fail-fast config load 2026-07-02) and confirmed first-and-only occurrence. Pin re-reviewed 2026-07-08: the file changed by an ordinary regen (call-agent spec_name tier wording) — the historical claim is unaffected.

gaps.impact: Shipped SKILL.md content varies with the generating machine's substrate state: a mid-migration or legacy substrate silently truncates the documented surface (realized once); a substrate with tool_operations entries would publish project configuration as package documentation (latent). Exit-0-on-crash means neither case is detectable at generation time — the false-pass mode the project's own doctrine names as fallback-swallows-failure, in the toolchain itself.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Hermetic generation plus fail-loud: execute factories against an isolated empty cwd so SKILL.md is a pure function of the package (runtime substrate coupling stays in the runtime, per FEAT-010; the generator deliberately documents zero environment-derived registrations), and with the environment isolated, a factory throw loses its "expected" justification — the generator exits nonzero instead of emitting partial output. Requires checking pi-behavior-monitors' three-tier discovery under a hermetic cwd (examples tier should still document). Determination at task time; this filing fixes the class, not the incident (already corrected at 6d91cb0).

gaps.title: Versioned substrate documents never converge their persisted schema_version at write time — config is the live instance: reads migrate in memory forever, writes persist the stale version back
gaps.description: Read-time migration (loadConfigForDir, TASK-070) carries a lagging config forward in memory only; no write path stamps the current schema version, so the staleness is permanent: writeConfigForDir (context.ts:872-874) passes schema_version through verbatim, every sanctioned mutation writes 1.0.0 back (observed: amendConfigEntryForDir on a live-copy — on-disk 1.0.0 before load, after chain-walked load, and after the write), every load resolves the chain forever, and future bumps stack hops on every read. Fresh substrates are born lagging: writeSkeletonConfig writes a literal "1.0.0" (context.ts:948) and adoptConception writes conception.json's "1.0.0" against the 1.7.0 bundled schema. Scope hole confirmed against filed text: TASK-070 criterion 3 pins "carried forward in memory" (read half only); TASK-073 criterion 2 stamps "the whole-block object ... on every block write" (blocks only); no FEAT-010 criterion states persisted config version converges. Class: read-time in-memory migration without write-time version convergence — blocks (remedy filed, TASK-073, planned), config (this instance, unfiled until now), migrations.json (latent sibling: appendMigrationDeclForDir carries the loaded envelope version forward verbatim, migrations-store.ts:208-219, and loadMigrationsFileForDir has no migration path; benign while file and schema both sit at 1.0.0).

evidence.reference: writeConfigForDir delegates to writeTypedFile with the caller's object verbatim — nothing anywhere sets schema_version; the AJV gate validates but does not stamp.

Observed write-path fact (scratch byte-copy of the live substrate): amendConfigEntryForDir load→mutate→write persisted schema_version 1.0.0 unchanged after a chain-walked load — the sanctioned write writes the stale version back.

writeSkeletonConfig writes a literal schema_version "1.0.0"; adoptConception writes samples/conception.json which itself declares "1.0.0" (line 2) against the bundled 1.7.0 schema — fresh substrates are born in the mismatch branch.

Latent class sibling: appendMigrationDeclForDir carries the loaded migrations.json envelope schema_version forward verbatim, and loadMigrationsFileForDir raw-validates with no migration path — reproduces this defect at the first migrations-schema bump.

runMigrations applies chain fns only; an identity decl returns data unchanged, so even the in-memory form keeps 1.0.0 — the chain is resolved on every load, permanently (loadConfigForDir consults the registry on every mismatch).

gaps.impact: The on-disk validity signal is permanently false (1.0.0 declared, 1.7.0 effective) — raw readers cannot distinguish stale from current; chain resolution is paid on every config read forever and grows with every future bump; the ceremony-seeding and recovery machinery becomes load-bearing steady state for a fact one write-time stamp would make durable; fresh catalog substrates begin life in the mismatch branch.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Stamp schema_version = the bundled config schema version inside writeConfigForDir before delegating to writeTypedFile — truthful by construction (the AJV gate validates the post-stamp object against the bundled schema; every mutating caller loads through the migrating loader first), symmetric with TASK-073's block-envelope design, and self-converging (the first sanctioned write persists the current version; subsequent loads version-match and never consult the registry). Also fixes born-lagging fresh substrates (skeleton + adoptConception writes stamp current). Optional in-memory hygiene: replace the identity decl with a declarative set of schema_version (mechanically supported today). migrations.json's latent sibling is named for the class, addressed when its schema first bumps or folded into TASK-073's implementation locus if the stamp lands generically in writeTypedFile. Determination at task time.
gaps.closed_by: TASK-073 / VER-063 — the write-time stamp landed at the generic writeTypedFile locus (the fold disposition this gap's resolution named), self-gated on the schema declaring a top-level schema_version + carrying a version, so every versioned-document write converges: blocks, config.json (writeConfigForDir delegates to writeTypedFile against the bundled schema), and migrations.json (migrations-store writes via writeTypedFile against the bundled migrations schema). Live-proven on the active substrate: the first sanctioned post-stamp config write (the update run's baseline advance) converged on-disk config.json schema_version 1.0.0 -> 1.8.0, after which loads version-match and never consult the chain; a subsequent identical-content amend-config was a byte-identical no-op (convergence durable). Born-lagging fresh substrates are fixed by the same mechanism (writeSkeletonConfig and adoptConception write through writeConfigForDir, stamped current at write). Config-write convergence is regression-locked in block-version-stamp.test.ts.

gaps.title: An operator-filed partial config migration chain bricks every ceremony with no remediation hint; recovery path exists but is undocumented
gaps.description: A `config` migration declaration whose toVersion stops short of the bundled schema version (e.g. 1.0.0→1.2.0 against bundled 1.7.0) is correctly never replaced by ceremony seeding (never-clobber), but then no chain resolves to the bundled version and every ceremony throws — init/accept-all/install each loadConfig after seeding, so the ceremony family cannot heal past an operator-filed partial edge. Recovery works (write-schema-migration --operation create for the missing edge; the op path loads no config, verified) but no surface documents it and the throw (`no path from 1.0.0 to 1.7.0 ... (stuck at '1.2.0')`) names no remediation. If FGAP-105's write-time version convergence lands, this pocket shrinks substantially — a converged substrate stops depending on chains in steady state — so the fix may fold into that work.

evidence.reference: resolve/runMigrations throw plain Error on an unresolvable chain; the message names the stuck version but no remediation (no mention of write-schema-migration or the missing edge).

Adversarial-probe reproduction (TASK-070 verification round, this session): partial (config,1.0.0→1.2.0) edge filed on a scratch substrate → ceremony seeding correctly appends nothing (never-replace honored) → init/accept-all/install all throw; recovery via write-schema-migration create 1.2.0→1.7.0 then green.

gaps.impact: A substrate carrying a stale partial config chain is unrecoverable through ceremony re-entry; the operator must already know the undocumented write-schema-migration recovery to proceed.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires determination: a remediation hint in the unresolvable-chain error (naming write-schema-migration and the missing edge) and/or documenting the recovery; interacts with TASK-071's recover path and with FGAP-105's write-time convergence (which retires steady-state chain dependence).

gaps.title: Raw config.json readers bypass the config migration layer — silent shape divergence once the first shape-changing config migration ships
gaps.description: Two readers parse config.json raw, skipping loadConfigForDir's migrate-then-validate: resolveOperationVocabulary (pi-agent-dispatch/src/capability-composer.ts:26, active substrate, fail-safe) and loadConfigForDirBestEffort (pi-context/src/context-sdk.ts:1443, non-active substrates). Harmless while only identity chains exist; the day a shape-changing `config` migration ships (map_each's declared consumer: the state_derivation delta), these see the un-migrated shape while loadConfig sees the migrated one — divergent reads of the same file, no throw, no signal. Class member of FGAP-105's raw-readers-cannot-tell-stale-from-current observation; distinct defect: these paths never migrate at all.

evidence.reference: resolveOperationVocabulary raw-parses the ACTIVE substrate's config.json (fail-safe try/catch), bypassing the migration-aware loader.
loadConfigForDirBestEffort raw-parses non-active substrates' config.json — best-effort by design, but shape-blind to migrations.

gaps.impact: Latent split-brain on config shape between the migration-aware and raw read paths; surfaces only when a non-identity config migration lands, far from this cause, with no error at the divergence point.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires determination: route both through the migration-aware loader (best-effort semantics preserved via try/catch) or document them as deliberately raw with a shape-floor contract. FGAP-105's write-time convergence narrows the exposure window (converged files carry the current shape on disk) but does not close it for lagging substrates.
gaps.closed_by: TASK-079 / VER-066 — the filed determination resolved to migration-aware routing (not document-as-raw), and both named readers were converted: resolveOperationVocabulary (pi-agent-dispatch capability-composer) reads the active substrate's config via loadConfigForDir with its fail-safe tightened to alignment (any load failure yields TOOL_OPERATION_DEFAULTS; a raw AJV-invalid config's tool_operations no longer apply), and loadConfigForDirBestEffort (pi-context context-sdk, foreign substrates) prefers loadConfigForDir with a two-tier degraded contract — raw-parse fallback on unresolvable/invalid so the foreign prefix invariant stays fed (mis-filed foreign ids keep degrading to dangling; the resolve-ref suite caught and forced this tier during implementation), null on absent/unparsable. No reader of config.json sees a different shape than loadConfig wherever a chain resolves. Merged fe9af165; both cells tested at both sites; live parity confirmed on the converged active substrate.

gaps.title: context-switch declares target_dir schema-required while --to_previous ignores it — the CLI refuses the flag-omitted form the op description implies
gaps.description: The context-switch op description says target_dir is ignored for --to_previous, but the parameter is schema-required and the CLI enforces schema.required (pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts:440-443), so `pi-context context-switch --to_previous true` without --target_dir gets a UsageError. The switch-back form works only with a dummy target_dir supplied — documentation and enforcement contradict each other.

evidence.reference: context-switch parameter schema requires target_dir; the description states it is ignored for to_previous.
The CLI enforces schema.required before dispatch — the documented flag-omitted switch-back form is refused with a UsageError.

gaps.impact: Operators following the op's own description hit a usage error on the switch-back form; the workaround (a dummy required-but-ignored flag) contradicts the surfaced contract.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires determination: conditional requirement (target_dir required unless to_previous) vs correcting the description to state target_dir must be supplied and is ignored. Surfaced by the TASK-070 round-3 re-audit as targeted-review information; op schema untouched by that branch.

gaps.title: No build-time gate flags a narrowing catalog BLOCK-schema diff — the additive/expand-contract gate covers config.schema.json only
gaps.description: TASK-072 (FGAP-097) gates non-additive diffs of config.schema.json at build time; issue-005 covers same-version resync re-validation at APPLY time. No filing gates a narrowing catalog block-schema diff (samples/schemas/*.schema.json — a removed/renamed field or a new required property) at build/authoring time, so a narrowing block-schema change ships in the catalog undetected until a substrate's update/resync hits it. Surfaced as a coverage hole by the critical-focus outline derivation (dimension B): the block-schema half of the additive discipline has apply-time detection only.

evidence.reference: The 18 catalog block schemas have no build-time additive gate; TASK-072's filed files[] names only packages/pi-context/schemas/config.schema.json.

gaps.impact: A narrowing block-schema catalog change lands silently and surfaces as blocked resyncs or invalid items on downstream substrates, far from the authoring change.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires determination: extend TASK-072's gate to walk all catalog block schemas (same additive/expand-contract rule, migration-paired exception) or file a sibling gate. Positioned with MILE-003's convergence work.

gaps.title: Raw-reader bypass class beyond config is unverified — no filing establishes whether block-data raw readers exist and closes them as a class
gaps.description: FGAP-107 files exactly two config.json raw readers. Whether raw readers of block-data *.json exist (parsing substrate block files without the validated read path, and — once TASK-073 activates block read-time migration — without migration awareness) has not been verified, and no filing closes the class either way. Surfaced as a coverage hole by the critical-focus outline derivation (dimension C): the class-level enumeration is the missing precondition to declaring the read side split-brain-free.

evidence.reference: FGAP-107's two filed instances are config-only; no enumeration of block-data raw readers exists in any filing.

gaps.impact: Unknown exposure: if block-data raw readers exist, TASK-073's read-time migration activation creates the same silent split-brain FGAP-107 files for config, with no tracking item.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires determination after enumeration: grep-enumerate all non-test JSON.parse readers of substrate block files across packages/, verdict each (validated-path / deliberate-raw-with-contract / defect), and file or close the class on the evidence. Positioned with MILE-003.

gaps.title: Review-enforced conventions have no structural forcing function as a class — all 12 conventions declare enforcement review or manual
gaps.description: Every convention in the conventions block declares enforcement "review" or "manual"; only FGAP-071 files the unenforced-ness of ONE convention (gap-arc-coherence). The class — a convention registry whose members are all honor-system while the framework's own doctrine treats manual enforcement as a gap (the filing-provenance and branch-discipline guards exist precisely because review-enforcement fails) — is unfiled. Surfaced as a coverage hole by the critical-focus outline derivation (dimension E).

evidence.reference: All shipped conventions carry enforcement review/manual; no invariant, hook, or gate consumes the conventions block's enforcement field.

gaps.impact: Convention compliance depends entirely on session discipline; violations (deviations this project has repeatedly experienced) surface only by later audit, never at act time.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires determination: per-convention triage of which are structurally enforceable (invariant / write-gate / build-gate per the filing-pipeline enforcement surface) vs genuinely review-only, with the enforcement field made consequential. Positioned with MILE-007.

gaps.title: CLI errors carry no remediation hints as a class — only FGAP-106 asks for one error's hint
gaps.description: FGAP-106 files the missing remediation hint on one error (the unresolvable config chain). No filing requires that CLI/op errors name their sanctioned remediation generally — which op un-bricks, which flag previews, which ceremony heals. The class: the error surface tells the operator what failed but not the sanctioned way out, in a system whose whole design is that every recovery has a sanctioned op. Surfaced as a coverage hole by the critical-focus outline derivation (dimension F).

evidence.reference: Representative instance (FGAP-106): the throw names the stuck version but no remediation op; the pattern generalizes across the error surface with no filing covering it.

gaps.impact: Operators (human or agent) hitting a refusal must already know the recovery op; errors dead-end sessions that the design intends to be self-recoverable.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Requires determination: an error-surface audit enumerating refusal/throw sites with a sanctioned-recovery op, adding the hint per site or via a shared error-shaping layer (the cli render/formatAjvError precedent). Positioned with MILE-008.

gaps.title: Edge orientation (which endpoint is stored parent vs child) is carried by neither the relation_type name nor any queryable metadata, and no surface signals a wrong-orientation guess — backwards writes and empty reads both fail silently across ordering, membership, and wildcard relation_types
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: edge-orientation transparency + symmetric wrong-orientation signaling (write-reject and read-signal)
gaps.description: The closure-table stores every relationship as a directed {parent, child} edge, but which endpoint is parent vs child is a convention that neither the relation_type canonical_id NAME nor any queryable metadata carries, and NO consuming surface signals a wrong-orientation guess — the failure is always silent, so backwards edges (write side) and empty results (read side) accumulate undetected. The only structural pin on orientation is the presence-gated source/target kind check (validateEdgeAgainstRegistry, context-sdk.ts:1590 parent->source_kinds / :1595 child->target_kinds), which constrains orientation ONLY when the two kinds differ AND both endpoints resolve; it is not a direction check. Consequently: (WRITE) assertEdgeValidForWrite (context-sdk.ts:1629-1636) accepts a name-faithful-but-backwards edge with no signal — the FGAP-007/TASK-027 task_depends_on_task footgun (name reads parent-depends-on-child while the deriver treats parent=prerequisite; corrected edge 5621c2a; the reverted fix a08e586). (READ) the walk primitives (context.ts:1550-1567 walk-ancestors / 1585-1602 walk-descendants) are bare direction-followers that return [] on a wrong-orientation guess, indistinguishable from 'no such relationship' — live: `walk-ancestors --id TASK-027 --relationType task_positioned_in_phase` returns data:[] while `context-walk-descendants --parentId TASK-027` returns PHASE-M5-WRITE-INTEGRITY, because task_positioned_in_phase stores parent=task/child=phase (member points up to container), opposite the name's containment intuition. (DERIVE) currentState (context-sdk.ts:801-927) and roadmap-plan.ts are correct-by-hardcode, so they consume a backwards edge silently rather than flag it. (VALIDATE) context-validate (context-sdk.ts:2179-, :2251) never inspects orientation. General class: orientation is an unenforced, unsurfaced convention on a directed edge, and every write/read/derive/validate surface fails silently on the wrong guess; FGAP-007 (write/ordering/same-kind) is one cell of this matrix, not the whole defect.

evidence.reference: validateEdgeAgainstRegistry: parent gated to source_kinds, child to target_kinds — the ONLY structural pin on orientation, and only when kinds differ and both endpoints resolve; not a direction check
assertEdgeValidForWrite checks registration + kind only; a name-faithful backwards ordering edge is accepted and persisted with no signal (the FGAP-007 write cell)
walk-ancestors / walk-descendants are bare parent/child direction-followers; a wrong-orientation query returns [] indistinguishable from no-such-relationship (the read cell)
currentState blocked/ready deriver hardcodes parent=prerequisite/child=dependent — consumes a backwards edge silently, does not flag orientation
context-validate endpoint/relation loops check registration, existence, kind, cycle — never orientation; no validate-time catch for a backwards edge or a silent-empty read

read-config --registry relation_types: item_derived_from_item is source_kinds:[*]/target_kinds:[*] so the kind check can NEVER fire — a fully unconstrained silent backwards write, strictly worse than task_depends_on_task; membership is internally inconsistent — feature_contains_story/story_includes_item store container=parent (source=container) while task_positioned_in_phase/phase_positioned_in_milestone store container=child (source=member), so any learned orientation heuristic is wrong for the sibling

the investigation of record: full relation_type x surface matrix, verbatim reproduction transcripts for both instances, the silent-failure cell inventory, the prior-art coverage map, and the class characterization (commit 147cdf5)

gaps.impact: Every directed edge in the substrate is exposed to silent mis-orientation on both sides. WRITE: a filer or agent following the relation_type name files a backwards ordering edge (task_depends_on_task and the same-kind siblings milestone_precedes_milestone / decision_supersedes_decision / research_supersedes_research / story_depends_on_story / requirement_depends_on_requirement) or an arbitrarily-oriented item_derived_from_item (no kind pin at all) with no write-time signal; the defect surfaces only in derived blocked/ready or lineage state, divorced from the write. READ: any walk/query against the wrong end returns empty, indistinguishable from a genuine absence, so a consumer silently under-reads the graph (the mechanism that made this project's own membership query return empty). The membership category's split container-orientation guarantees the hazard cannot be learned away by convention. No surface — write, read, derive, or validate — makes a wrong-orientation guess observable, so both backwards edges and missed reads accumulate substrate-wide undetected. Addressing only the task_depends_on_task cell (FGAP-007 alone) leaves the wildcard write, the read-side silent-empty, and the membership inconsistency live and invites duplicate sibling filings.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Make edge orientation explicit metadata on the relation_type and consume it SYMMETRICALLY so a wrong-orientation guess is never silent, on either side: (1) declare per-relation_type endpoint-role/orientation metadata (extending source_kinds/target_kinds) for every orientation-bearing relation_type — ordering, membership, and the wildcard item_derived_from_item — naming which stored endpoint is which semantic role; (2) WRITE: the append gate REJECTS a backwards orientation-bearing edge at write time with a message naming the expected orientation (never auto-orient — the reverted a08e586 always-swap inverted canonically-authored edges); (3) READ: a walk/query issued against the wrong end of an orientation-bearing relation ERRORS (or normalizes with an explicit signal) rather than returning silent-empty, so an absence is distinguishable from a wrong-direction guess; (4) reconcile the membership category's split container-orientation so the metadata is self-consistent across contains/includes/positioned_in; (5) optionally a validate-time check that flags any orientation-bearing edge whose stored direction violates its declared roles. The metadata is authored once and consumed by write, read, derive, and validate — the class closes when no orientation-bearing relation on any surface fails silently on the wrong guess. TASK-027 is re-scoped from the single task_depends_on_task write cell to this class-level fix; FGAP-007 is the write-ordering instance; FGAP-090/FGAP-093 (kind/existence parity, which defer direction here) are adjacent partial coverage.
gaps.closed_by: TASK-027 / VER-062 — role_direction ('as_parent'|'as_child') declared as the single orientation source of truth on the 17 role-bearing relation_types, consumed by the deriver / rollup / roadmap / promote (the task_gated_by_item + roadmap module-constant + item_derived_from_item string-literal hardcodes deleted); role-typed --primary/--counter write authoring with ambiguous-bare-append reject for same-kind/wildcard relations (never auto-oriented); read-side wrong-orientation walk signal for disjoint-kind relations (throws naming the counterpart op) with same-kind/wildcard honestly returning []; config schema advanced to 1.8.0 with the active .context substrate migrated (chain + role_direction). Merged 3bc3d7a4; build/check/test green; runtime demos + fresh-context adversarial probe PASS; deriver output byte-identical to the pre-change baseline; context-validate warnings-only baseline. FGAP-007 (write/ordering/same-kind) and the task_positioned_in_phase read silent-empty are the closed instances.

gaps.title: context-validate performs no block-item schema validation — a substrate with schema-invalid items reports clean, so resync-introduced invalidity persists undetected until a write to that block fails
gaps.description: validateContext (context-sdk.ts:2233) checks edge integrity (endpoint resolution, relation_type registration, kind membership, cycles via validateRelations), config-declared invariants (requires-edge, status-consistency), and SoT-registry drift — it never AJV-validates any block's items against the block's installed schema. Schema validity is enforced only at write time (writeTypedFile validates before landing) and by the per-block validate-block-items op, which no composed surface runs substrate-wide. Consequence: items that become invalid WITHOUT a write to their block — the live class instance is a same-version catalog resync that narrows the installed schema under an item set that no longer conforms — are invisible to context-validate, which reports clean/warnings while the block is invalid. The boundary is even documented as a known absence in STORY-005's filed text ('which update doesn't expose and context-validate doesn't do') but was never filed as a gap, and every closure/verification surface that cites a clean context-validate over-reads the verdict as covering schema validity. Live instance: after the catalog story-schema resync, validate-block-items --block story reports all 14 items invalid (additionalProperties: user_kind undeclared) while context-validate, run the same minute, reported warnings-only with zero story findings. With envelope schema_version stamping now live, the invalidity surfaces as a hard write failure at the NEXT story.json write — loud, but far from the resync that caused it and only when a write happens to occur.

evidence.reference: validateContext JSDoc + body enumerate its full coverage: edge integrity, config-declared invariants, SoT drift, merged validateRelations diagnostics — no block-item AJV pass exists anywhere in the function

live instance: validate-block-items --block story -> valid:false with 14 additionalProperties failures (every STORY item carries user_kind; the resynced installed schema does not declare it under additionalProperties:false), while context-validate run in the same session reported warnings-only with zero story findings

STORY-005 filed text documents the boundary as known: 'I can validate the block items directly against the target catalog schema and see what fails — which update doesn't expose and context-validate doesn't do'

the producing cause of the live instance is issue-005's same-version resync verbatim-overwrite arm (no item re-validation); this gap is the detection-side complement — the damage a silent producer creates is also invisible to the integrity verdict

gaps.impact: The substrate's one composed integrity verdict does not mean what its consumers take it to mean: closures, verification criteria, session gates, and CI-style checks cite 'context-validate clean' as substrate health, but a block can sit fully schema-invalid behind that clean. Invalidity introduced without a block write (schema resync, hand edits, catalog drift) accumulates undetected; with envelope version stamping live, it detonates at the next write to the affected block — a hard failure divorced in time and place from the cause. The live story block is currently in exactly this state: 14 invalid items, write-bricked, integrity verdict clean.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Give the composed validate surface a substrate-wide schema-validity pass: for each installed block, validate the whole block (envelope + items) against its installed schema — migration-aware for stamped envelopes (the existing validateBlockWithMigrationForDir), raw for unstamped — and surface failures as errors in the context-validate result (per-item id/field/keyword, the validate-block-items shape). Whether the pass lives inside validateContext, or as a composed sibling the context-validate op always runs, is the implementing task's mechanism choice; the requirement is that a clean context-validate verdict actually covers schema validity. Pairs with issue-005 (the producer-side fix: same-version resync must re-validate like the bump path); the pair closes both ends of the class (silent introduction, silent persistence).
gaps.closed_by: TASK-078 / VER-065 — the filed resolution shipped: validateContext gained the substrate-wide block schema-validity sweep (every block with an installed schema + block file validated whole, migration-aware for stamped envelopes via validateBlockWithMigrationForDir, raw otherwise), surfacing per-item failures as errors (block_schema_invalid, item id from the AJV instancePath, field/keyword); pointer-less projects skip gracefully. Live-proven on the active substrate: the sweep reported the previously-invisible 14 invalid story items (status invalid) that the pre-fix verdict missed, and returned to the warnings-only baseline after the paired producer fix + catalog story user_kind remediation resynced the block to validity. The producer side (same-version resync re-validation) landed in the same task, closing both ends of the class. Merged 5dc2f2fc.

gaps.title: resync/resolve blocked pipeline classifies every throw as item-validation failure — non-validation refusals are mislabeled, mark valid blocks, dead-end the routed remedy, and break dry/live parity
gaps.description: resyncSchema's version-bump catch converts ANY throw into blocked with reason validation-failed and a synthetic failure ({instancePath:'', keyword:'error', message:String(err)}), and every downstream consumer trusts that classification. Observed end-to-end on the pre-identity reproduction (writeBlockForDir's substrateIdForDir throw, a pure infrastructure precondition): (1) blockedDetail tells the operator to fix items that are valid — the infrastructure message is costumed as a validation failure; (2) git-style failure markers are inscribed INTO the fully-valid block file and a pending-blocked record is persisted; (3) the routed remedy resolve-blocked re-validates (passes) then THROWS UNCAUGHT mid-commit on the same precondition, leaving partial state — schema advanced to the target version, markers stripped, block unwritten/unstamped, pending entry stale — the only known violation of the per-component byte-exact refusal discipline; (4) the closed dry/live parity guarantee is silently violated for this class (simulateResyncOutcome models migration + re-validation only, never write preconditions: dry predicts migrated, live blocks). Class: refusal-cause conflation — the pipeline has exactly one refusal category (item-validation) while throws at the write boundary include non-validation causes; the pre-identity stamping throw is the triggering instance, and any future non-validation throw at that boundary (I/O failure, corrupt sidecar) inherits all four facets.

evidence.reference: the version-bump catch: err instanceof ValidationError ? mapValidationFailures(...) : synthetic {keyword:'error'} — both land under reason 'validation-failed'; marker inscription + pending-blocked follow for the live path

resolveBlocked's commit sequence (schema write, marker-strip raw write, writeBlockForDir, base advance, pending clear) runs OUTSIDE its validation try — a stamping throw aborts mid-commit; observed: schema 1.0.1 + markers stripped + block unwritten + pending stale
two-cell reproduction (substrate_id presence the only variable) + the resolve-blocked partial-commit run — all four facets observed

gaps.impact: An operator hit by any non-validation refusal is sent to fix items that are fine, has failure markers written into a valid block, and the prescribed remedy corrupts the refusal's all-or-nothing guarantee. Diagnosis cost is high precisely because the surface lies about the cause class.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Classify at the catch: distinguish item-validation failure (err instanceof ValidationError) from non-validation refusal, carry the distinction through blockedDetail (a truthful reason vocabulary), and gate the validation-only consequences (marker inscription, pending-blocked routing) on the validation class; resolve-blocked's commit sequence gains the same all-or-nothing discipline as resyncSchema (no partial commit on a write-boundary throw). Dry/live parity for the class follows derivatively once the ceremony-entry identity establishment decision removes the pre-identity instance; the classification fix stands for every other non-validation throw at the boundary.
gaps.closed_by: TASK-081 / VER-069 — refusal-cause classification landed at both resyncSchema catch sites: an AJV ValidationError keeps reason validation-failed (per-item failures, markers, pending record — unchanged); any other throw reports the new reason write-failed with the thrown message carried, and the validation-only consequences never fire (no markers inscribed into the block, no pending-blocked record persisted). renderBlocked renders write-failed truthfully (the items were not flagged invalid; address the precondition, re-run update) and its trailing fix-items guidance is gated to reports carrying a pending-persisting entry. resolveBlocked's commit is all-or-nothing: pre-commit bytes of every touched file (migrations.json, installed schema, block, config.json, pending record) captured and byte-restored on any commit-phase throw, returning resolved:false with the truthful failure — the partial-commit facet is closed. The dry/live parity facet for the pre-identity instance is deferred by design to the ceremony-entry identity establishment implementation (the enacted decision), which removes the instance; the classification stands for every other non-validation throw at the boundary.
